Next I started creating the public subnets and connected them to the new vpc that is created, also it creates an IP address ranges of the following subnets.I also added tags for easier identification.

Then I also created Private subnets and associate it to my new vpc. Also created an IP address range of the following subnets and also provided names for easier identification.By creating private subnet it helps in keeping our resources private and the subnets that want to be public making it accessible from the internet.

Next we move on by getting the resource block to create route tables and it’s routes our subnets to the vpc I got this from the terraform registry.We associate the routes to all Ipv4 traffic and it’s being directed to the internet gateway.

Now we need to associate the route table and the subnets to the new VPC.This process will ensure that route tables can route there traffic with the proper subnets having proper ec2 instance communication.

Next we can create the Internet Gateway this will allow our inbound and outbound internet traffic to reach the instances and to the web.

Next we started creating the security groups from the terraform resource block I gave it a name and connected it to the vpc that we created earlier.We also want to associate the posts that we will need open and it allows HTTP,and SSH traffic.This carries out any IP Address traffic and send it to all outbound traffic.

Next I also created a security group another resource security block which is for mySQL. We gave our security group a name connected the security group to the VPC.I allowed the necessary ports on my inbound creating all IP traffic and setting the egress(outbound). This carries out proper communication.

# Creating EC2 Instance 2resource "aws_instance" "Ec2_instances2" {
  ami                    = "ami-xxxxxxxxxx" # Change It to your own AMI
  instance_type          = "t2.micro"
  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.web_sg.id]
  subnet_id              = aws_subnet.public_subnet_2.id
  key_name               = "Ec2Kp"
  user_data              = base64encode(file("userdata_script.sh"))    name = "Ec2_instance2"}

We can start creating the Ec2 instances and I got the resource block from the terraform registry, we need to specify the right ami that we want to use and in this case I went with amazon Linux, next we define the instance type t2 micro. Also set the network configurations and connected them to the specified security group names.Then I also placed the subnets and provided a key pair.I already had a key pair so I will be reusing it.

#!/bin/bashyum update -ysystemctl start httpd echo 'Hooray The Test Page Is Working :)' | sudo tee -a /var/www/html/index.html

Next I provided a user data script and linked it to my instance so that anytime we launch the instances the script is executed. Then to end it off I added tags on our instance.

Next we can start creating our Relational Database and it’s being connected to the VPC that we created.The RDS instance runs with MySQL it will identify the network configuration,security group, and the subent.

Then we can create another resource block that specifies the private subnets to the rds database.This ensure that the subnets are private to the database .

terraform initterraform validateterraform planterraform destroy

We can now navigate back to the AWS Console to see that our following resources are now created like our VPC.

Next we can see our subnets are created successfully and connected to the route tables and the main Internet Gateway.

We can also see that the Internet Gateway was also created.

We can also see that the security groups are also created.

We can also see that the database was also created and is available.

Next we can see that our ec2 instance is also created and running.Also the security groups is associated to the instance.

Next we can copy the public IP from one of our running instances and paste it into another web browser and we are prompted with our running test page successfully.

# Configure the AWS Providerterraform {    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"  }  region = "us-east-1"
# Create a VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)
resource "aws_vpc" "Trevorvpc" {
  cidr_block       = "10.0.0.0/16"
  instance_tenancy = "default"    Name = "Trevorvpc"}
# Public Subnets for Web Server Tier
resource "aws_subnet" "public_subnet_1" {
  vpc_id                  = aws_vpc.Trevorvpc.id
  cidr_block              = "10.0.1.0/24"
  availability_zone       = "us-east-1a"
  map_public_ip_on_launch = true    Name = "PublicSubnet-1"}
resource "aws_subnet" "public_subnet_2" {
  vpc_id                  = aws_vpc.Trevorvpc.id
  cidr_block              = "10.0.2.0/24"
  availability_zone       = "us-east-1b"
  map_public_ip_on_launch = true
    Name = "PublicSubnet-2"}
# Private Subnets for RDS Tier
resource "aws_subnet" "private_subnet_1" {
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.Trevorvpc.id
  cidr_block        = "10.0.3.0/24"
  availability_zone = "us-east-1a"
    Name = "PrivateSubnet-1"}
resource "aws_subnet" "private_subnet_2" {
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.Trevorvpc.id
  cidr_block        = "10.0.4.0/24"
  availability_zone = "us-east-1b"    Name = "PrivateSubnet-2"}resource "aws_route_table" "private_route_table" {
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.Trevorvpc.id
}
resource "aws_route" "private_route" {
  route_table_id         = aws_route_table.private_route_table.id
  destination_cidr_block = "0.0.0.0/0"
  gateway_id             = aws_internet_gateway.IGW.idresource "aws_route_table" "public_route_table" {
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.Trevorvpc.id
}
resource "aws_route" "public_route" {
  route_table_id         = aws_route_table.public_route_table.id
  destination_cidr_block = "0.0.0.0/0"
  gateway_id             = aws_internet_gateway.IGW.id
# Public Route Table Association
resource "aws_route_table_association" "routetableassociation1" {
  subnet_id      = aws_subnet.public_subnet_1.id
  route_table_id = aws_route_table.public_route_table.id
resource "aws_route_table_association" "routetableassociation2" {
  subnet_id      = aws_subnet.public_subnet_2.id
  route_table_id = aws_route_table.public_route_table.id
}
# Private Route Table Association
resource "aws_route_table_association" "privaterouteassociation1" {
  subnet_id      = aws_subnet.private_subnet_1.id
  route_table_id = aws_route_table.private_route_table.id
resource "aws_route_table_association" "privaterouteassociation2" {
  subnet_id      = aws_subnet.private_subnet_2.id
  route_table_id = aws_route_table.private_route_table.id
}
# Create an Internet Gateway
resource "aws_internet_gateway" "IGW" {
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.Trevorvpc.id
    Name = "MainIGW"}
# Creating a Security Group for Web Server
resource "aws_security_group" "web_sg" {  description = "Allow inbound HTTP and SSH"
  vpc_id      = aws_vpc.Trevorvpc.id
    from_port   = 80    protocol    = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }    from_port   = 22    protocol    = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }    from_port   = 0    protocol    = "-1"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }
# Security Group for RDS MySQL
resource "aws_security_group" "rds_sg" {  description = "Allow inbound MySQL"
  vpc_id      = aws_vpc.Trevorvpc.id
    from_port   = 3306    protocol    = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }    from_port   = 0    protocol    = "-1"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }
# Creating EC2 Instance 1 
resource "aws_instance" "Ec2_instances1" {
  ami                    = "ami-085ad6ae776d8f09c" # Change It to your own AMI
  instance_type          = "t2.micro"
  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.web_sg.id]
  subnet_id              = aws_subnet.public_subnet_1.id
  key_name               = "Ec2Kp"
  user_data              = base64encode(file("userdata_script.sh"))    name = "Ec2_instance1"}
# Creating EC2 Instance 2
resource "aws_instance" "Ec2_instances2" {
  ami                    = "ami-085ad6ae776d8f09c" # Change It to your own AMI
  instance_type          = "t2.micro"
  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.web_sg.id]
  subnet_id              = aws_subnet.public_subnet_2.id
  key_name               = "Ec2Kp"
  user_data              = base64encode(file("userdata_script.sh"))    name = "Ec2_instance2"}resource "aws_db_instance" "mydb" {
  identifier             = "mydb"
  engine                 = "mysql"
  engine_version         = "5.7"
  instance_class         = "db.t3.micro"
  allocated_storage      = 20
  db_name                = "trevordatabase"
  username               = "admin"
  password               = "password"
  db_subnet_group_name   = aws_db_subnet_group.dbmain.name
  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.rds_sg.id]
  multi_az               = false
  storage_type           = "gp2"
  skip_final_snapshot    = true
}
# Database for Subnet Group
resource "aws_db_subnet_group" "dbmain" {
  name       = "main-subnet-group"
  subnet_ids = [aws_subnet.private_subnet_1.id, aws_subnet.private_subnet_2.id]    Name = "MainDataBase"}

Conclusion: Thank you for taking your time to read through my documentation. I’ve really enjoyed deploying the following resources with terraform making it easy and quicker for your resources to work you can defiantly scale the resources if you need to .

items.title: How HTTPS Works: A Deep Dive Into Secure Web Communication
items.author: Ahmed Adel
items.content:
Imagine you’re sending a secret letter to your friend across the city. If you hand it to a random messenger without protection, anyone can read or even alter it before it reaches your friend. This is what happens when data is sent over the internet without encryption — it’s vulnerable to eavesdroppers and hackers.

Now, what if you placed the letter in a locked box and only your friend had the key? No one else could read it. This is the essence of encryption — securing communication so only the intended recipient can access the information.

But there’s still a problem: how do you safely share the key to unlock the box? If a spy intercepts it, they can read everything. To solve this, we use a special trick — two keys: one for locking and another for unlocking, ensuring the message stays safe. This is how HTTPS protects online communication, making sure your sensitive information — like passwords and credit card details — remains secure.

Symmetric key algorithms use the same key for both encryption and decryption. Examples include AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) and DES (Data Encryption Standard).

Asymmetric Key Algorithms

Asymmetric key algorithms use a pair of keys: a public key for encryption and a private key for decryption. It is computationally infeasible to derive the private key from the public key. Examples include RSA (Rivest-Shamir-Adleman) and Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC).

The public key is one of the key pairs used in asymmetric encryption. It can be freely shared with anyone.

The private key is the other half of the asymmetric key pair. It must be kept secret, as it is used to decrypt messages encrypted with the corresponding public key.

Hashing algorithms take any input and generate a fixed-length hash value. Hashes are one-way functions, meaning they cannot be reversed to obtain the original data. Examples include SHA-256, SHA-512, and MD5 (though MD5 is considered vulnerable to attacks).

A digital signature is a cryptographic technique used to verify the integrity and authenticity of digital documents. The signing process involves:

1. Hashing the document’s content using a hashing algorithm.

2. Encrypting the hash using the document owner’s private key.

3. Sending the document along with the encrypted hash.

1. Decrypt the encrypted hash using the sender’s public key.

2. Hash the received document and compare the two hashes.

3. If the hashes match, the document is verified as unaltered and authentic.

Certificate Authorities (CAs)

Certificate Authorities (CAs) are trusted organizations responsible for issuing and signing digital certificates using their private key. Their public keys are pre-installed in operating systems and browsers to verify digital certificates.

When a client and a server communicate over HTTPS, they must encrypt the transmitted data. There are two options for encryption:

1. Symmetric encryption, which is fast but requires both parties to share a secret key.

2. Asymmetric encryption, which solves the key-sharing issue but is slower.

To balance security and performance, HTTPS uses asymmetric encryption only for securely exchanging the symmetric key. Once the symmetric key is securely shared, all further communication is encrypted using symmetric encryption.

The Issue with Symmetric Encryption

In symmetric encryption, both the client and server must share the same key for encryption and decryption. However, transmitting this key over an insecure network poses a major risk — attackers could intercept it and decrypt the data.

How HTTPS Solves This Problem

HTTPS solves this issue by using asymmetric encryption to securely exchange the symmetric key. After that, symmetric encryption is used for all subsequent communication.

Step-by-Step Example of HTTPS in Action

1. SSL Certificate Generation

  • The server owner generates an SSL/TLS certificate by providing its public key and organization details to a Certificate Authority (CA).
  • The CA signs the certificate using its private key and issues it to the server owner.

2. Client Requests the Server’s SSL Certificate

  • When a client (e.g., a web browser) connects to an HTTPS server, it requests the server’s SSL certificate.
  • The server responds by sending its SSL certificate, which contains its public key.

3. Client Verifies the SSL Certificate

  • The client verifies the certificate by:
  • Hashing the server’s public key.
  • Decrypting the certificate’s digital signature using the CA’s public key (pre-installed in the OS/browser).
  • Comparing the hashes. If they match, the certificate is valid and has not been tampered with.

4. Securely Exchanging the Symmetric Key

  • The client generates a pre-master secret (a temporary key used to derive the symmetric encryption key).
  • The client encrypts the pre-master secret using the server’s public key and sends it to the server.
  • The server decrypts the pre-master secret using its private key.
  • Both the client and server use the pre-master secret to generate the symmetric session key.
  • Now, both the client and server have a shared symmetric key.
  • They use this key to encrypt and decrypt all subsequent communication.
  • This ensures secure, high-speed data exchange between the client and server.

HTTPS secures data transmission by combining asymmetric and symmetric encryption. It uses asymmetric encryption only for securely exchanging the symmetric key, and then switches to symmetric encryption for performance. This ensures that sensitive information remains private and tamper-proof during communication over the internet.

items.title: Build a Beginner-Friendly AWS DevOps Pipeline From Scratch (With Real-Time Security Scans)
items.author: Harikrishnan M
items.content:
Welcome to this step-by-step guide on setting up a basic DevOps pipeline on AWS. We’ll use AWS services — specifically , , , , and  — to create a smooth path from source code to a live containerized application. We’ll also enable Amazon ECR’s vulnerability scanning to keep our container images as secure as possible.

By the end of this tutorial, you’ll have a fully automated solution that detects new code commits, builds a Docker image, scans it for vulnerabilities, and deploys it to Amazon ECS Fargate. Let’s dive in!

  • Ensure you have an (not the root account) with permissions to create and manage:
  • CodeCommit, CodeBuild, CodePipeline
  • Amazon ECS clusters and services
  • Related IAM roles and policies
  • For a quick start, you can use an IAM user with (though in production, you’d want more granular permissions).

(Optional but recommended)

It helps with testing Docker logins and managing resources. Install AWS CLI.

  • Understanding Docker containers will help. We’ll provide minimal Docker instructions here, but any Docker basics tutorial would be a good supplement.

Step 1: Create a Repository in AWS CodeCommit

Instead of using GitHub, we’ll store our code in , a fully-managed source control service.

  1. Provide a (e.g., aws-devops-demo).
  2. (Optional) Add a and .

Once created, you’ll see instructions for connecting to the repository using HTTPS or SSH.

Clone the new repository to your local machine:

git clone https://git-codecommit.<YOUR-REGION>.amazonaws.com/v1/repos/aws-devops-democd aws-devops-demo

Step 2: Set Up a Simple Node.js Application

Let’s create a minimal Node.js app that responds with a simple message.

In the aws-devops-demo folder, create a file named app.js with the following content:

const http = require('http');const port = process.env.PORT || 3000;
const requestHandler = (request, response) => {
  response.end('Hello from AWS DevOps Demo!');
const server = http.createServer(requestHandler);
server.listen(port, () => {
  console.log(`Server running on port ${port}`);

Initialize a Node.js project:

  1. This will create a default package.json file with basic information.
  2. Add and commit your changes:
  • git add . git commit -m "Initial commit with Node.js app" git push

This pushes your code to the CodeCommit repository.

Step 3: Create a Dockerfile

We’ll containerize our Node.js application so it can run on ECS Fargate.

In the aws-devops-demo folder, create a file named Dockerfile:

# Use official Node.js LTS image as a parent imageFROM node:14
# Set the working directory
WORKDIR /usr/src/app
# Copy package.json and package-lock.json for caching
COPY package*.json ./RUN npm install
# Copy the rest of the application files
COPY . .EXPOSE 3000CMD ["node", "app.js"]
git add Dockerfilegit commit -m "Add Dockerfile for containerization"

Step 4: Create an Amazon ECR Repository (With Image Scanning Enabled)

We’ll store our Docker image in (Elastic Container Registry) and enable vulnerability scanning to help keep it secure.

  1. : aws-devops-demo.
  2. : Enable to automatically scan images for vulnerabilities.

Make note of the repository URI, something like:

123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/aws-devops-demo

Step 5: Create a Build Project in AWS CodeBuild

will build and push our Docker image to ECR.

  1. Click .
  2. : aws-devops-demo-build.
  3. : Select .
  • : aws-devops-demo.
  • : main (or your preferred branch).
  • Environment image: .
  • Operating system: .
  • Image: aws/codebuild/standard:latest.
  • Service role: Create a new one or use an existing one. Make sure it has permission to push images to ECR.
  • You can either write your commands inline in the console or create a buildspec.yml in your repo. Let’s create a buildspec.yml file in our repository for best practices.

Open your local repo, create a file named buildspec.yml, and add:

version: 0.2phases:    commands:
      - echo Logging in to Amazon ECR...
      - aws ecr get-login-password --region us-east-1 | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com
      - REPOSITORY_URI=123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/aws-devops-demo
      - IMAGE_TAG=$CODEBUILD_RESOLVED_SOURCE_VERSION    commands:
      - echo Building the Docker image...
      - docker build -t $REPOSITORY_URI:$IMAGE_TAG .
      - echo Pushing the Docker image...
      - docker push $REPOSITORY_URI:$IMAGE_TAG    commands:
      - echo Build completed on `date`
      - echo Creating imagedefinitions.json file...
      - printf '[{"name":"container-name","imageUri":"%s"}]' $REPOSITORY_URI:$IMAGE_TAG > imagedefinitions.json
artifacts:    - imagedefinitions.json
  • 123456789012 with your AWS account ID
  • us-east-1 with your AWS Region
  • aws-devops-demo with your ECR repo name if it’s different
  1. Commit and push buildspec.yml:
  • git add buildspec.yml git commit -m "Add buildspec for CodeBuild" git push

In the creation screen, under “Buildspec”, select .

  • : buildspec.yml
  • Select/create an S3 bucket to store the build artifacts.

Click .

Step 6: Create an ECS Cluster

We’ll run our containerized application on using , which eliminates the need to manage servers.

  1. Select (Fargate).
  2. Give it a (e.g., aws-devops-demo-cluster).

Step 7: Create a Task Definition and Service

We need a to tell ECS how to run our container, and a to keep it running.

  1. In the , go to → Create new Task Definition.
  2. : aws-devops-demo-task.
  3. : Leave default or pick one if required for your app.
  4. : Choose the ECS task execution role that allows pulling images from ECR.
    1. : Click .
    • : container-name (this must match the name in our buildspec.yml’s imagedefinitions.json).
    • : For now, you can put a placeholder like 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/aws-devops-demo:latest.
    • : 3000 → 3000 (TCP).
    1. Go to your new → → .
    2. : aws-devops-demo-service.
    3. : 1 (for demo).
    4. : Choose the default VPC and subnets or any custom ones you have.
    5. : Ensure it allows inbound traffic on port 3000 (or whichever port your container is listening on).
    6. : You can either skip or set up an Application Load Balancer. For simplicity, skip for now, but for production, it’s recommended to use a load balancer.
    7. Click , then .

    You now have a container service waiting to be updated with the latest Docker image from ECR.

    Step 8: Create a CodePipeline

    will orchestrate the entire CI/CD flow:

    • Listen for changes in CodeCommit
    • Trigger CodeBuild to build/push the Docker image
    • Deploy the new image to ECS
    1. : aws-devops-demo-pipeline.
    2. : Create a new role or use an existing one.
    3. : Use the default S3 location or specify a new one.
    • : AWS CodeCommit
    • : aws-devops-demo
    • CodePipeline will detect changes to this repository.
    • : AWS CodeBuild
    • : aws-devops-demo-build
    • : Amazon ECS
    • : aws-devops-demo-cluster
    • : aws-devops-demo-service
    • : imagedefinitions.json
    • This tells CodePipeline to grab the imagedefinitions.json generated by CodeBuild and update the ECS service with the new image URI.

    Click . The pipeline will automatically run once you finish.

    Step 9: Test the Pipeline

    1. Pull the source from CodeCommit.
    2. Build the Docker image using CodeBuild and push it to ECR.
    • With enabled, ECR will scan the image for vulnerabilities.
    1. Deploy the new container image to your ECS service.

    in the CodePipeline console. Within a few minutes, you should see at each stage.

    Step 10: Verify the Deployment

    • Go to the → Clusters → aws-devops-demo-cluster → Services → aws-devops-demo-service.
    • Check the tab to see the running tasks.
    • If you set up a load balancer, navigate to the load balancer’s DNS name in your browser. Otherwise, find the public IP of the Fargate task’s ENI in the EC2 console or the ECS task details and go to http://PUBLIC_IP:3000.
    • Go to → Repositories → aws-devops-demo → click on the latest image → select .
    • If any vulnerabilities are found, remediate them in your code or Dockerfile and push an updated image.

    Step 11: Make a Code Change to See the Pipeline in Action

    Test the automation by changing the message in app.js:

    response.end('Hello from AWS DevOps Demo 2.0!');
    git add app.jsgit commit -m "Update response message"

    will detect the new commit, trigger a fresh build, push the updated image, and deploy it to ECS. After deployment, refresh your ECS service endpoint to see the updated message.

    Congratulations! You’ve built a fully automated DevOps pipeline using AWS services from scratch. Here’s what you set up:

    • for source control.
    • to store Docker images (with vulnerability scanning).
    • to build and push Docker images.
    • to tie everything together into an automated CI/CD workflow.
    • (Fargate) to run your containerized application.

items.title: Why and how you want to run Grafana in Kiosk Mode
items.author: Kurt Thomas Steinert
items.content:
Running automation surely has it’s advantages like less repetitive tasks and stable execution timings. However it also has it’s own set of personal nightmares for the people who are responsible for ensuring the automation is doing what it should. Be it autoscaling of computing instances or running automated tests for a release cycle — when heavily relied on systems fail this can provoke a cascade of shit storms creating a constant tension which will keep you from sleeping well or enjoying your free time.

To prevent this tension and to ensure you know how your supervised systems are doing, there are multiple solutions to this problem. One of this solution is to have a monitor in your perimeter showing you the most important information. Why does this help? Because the human brain is very good in pattern matching. You may notice unregularities in your dashboards from which you can predict future outtakes on a level which can hardly be mapped by alerting rules.

Maybe AI is able to predict on the same or even a better level. But when AI reaches this level, probably there will be no humans left anymore to acknowledge this so who cares.

For the kiosk mode to be helpful Grafana brings a nice feature called which shows multiple dashboards one after another in a rolling fashion.

If you are an automation engineer you may want to see an overview of the latest CI task metrics and some graphes of the performance over time. Everything that helps you getting a better understanding of your field of responsibility will help you be prepared for incident management (e.g. screaming managers) or effective priorization.

The latter will translate into maximizing the amount of not executed work which is a cornerstone of automation. And remember — automation engineers that are not currently working on something are a good sign since everything works as expected. They may at the same time be thinking about the next batch of things they can automate.

STFU, give me the manual for setting this stuff up already

Yeah, in case I got your brain heated and your hands soggy let me show you how to create a fast and easy setup that gets you running withing minutes.

  • A semi modern pc with network capabilities (called kiosk)
  • Keyboard/Mouse for initial setup
  • Public ssh key from your main workstation for accessing the pc over network
  • Ubuntu Desktop installation medium (like an USB stick)

For a base we start with Ubuntu Desktop as operating system. Why? Because it’s the defacto standard for desktop users all over the world, it has a huge community and a good documentation. You will find answers to all your (project related) problems on the internet if you run into troubles. As hardware stack we need some semi modern pc or laptop with network capabability (I use wifi) and a monitor. Peripherie like keyboard and mouse is only necessary for the initial setup since everything else we will do via ssh. To keep things simple we will call the pc where the kiosk mode runs on the . Crazy creativity working here.

After we installed Ubuntu and created a user, we login, open up the settings menu and activate the for our user (as seen in figure 1). Note that you need to unlock this setting by entering the users sudo password.

Next we will add our public ssh key from our main workstation in the users authorized keys file on the kiosk (usually found under /home/$USER/.ssh/authorized_keys). Just to make sure everything we need works as expected we can check if the ssh server is configured (/etc/ssh/sshd_config) and enabled to start during boot ( systemctl enable ssh.service).

We can now give back the peripherie to our flatmate so they can continue to waste time on social media. The rest of the receipt we will do from our main workstation.

So we log in to the kiosk via ssh, sudo into the root account and finally remove all the unnecessary baggage called ubuntu-desktop (or ubuntu-desktop-minimal if the minimal version has been installed) followed by an autoremove of unnecessary packages ( apt autoremove --purge ). Afterwards we install openbox with apt install openbox. Since this is the only window manager left on the device, it should automatically be used on auto login.

Before we can finish our setup we need to get the shared url of a playlist from Grafana. Let me show you how to get this. Open up your grafana instance in your browser.

On the next screen we can create a new playlist where we can add as many dashboards as we like. When we are done we save the playlist and find ourselfes back on the playlist overview screen. From here we can get the share url.

The last part of this magically soothing setup experience is to configure the openbox autostart for our user. For this we create a user local bin directory (mkdir ~/.local/bin) and put our kiosk.sh script into this. It’s important that you run this as the user itself and NOT as the root user.

#!/bin/bash
KIOSK_URL="https://grafana.com/playlists/" # put your Grafana kiosk url here
xset -dpms     # Disable DPMS (Energy Star) features
xset s off     # Disable screensaver
xset s noblank # Don't blank video deviceunclutter -display 0:0 -noevents -grab &
# Wait for the network connection to be ready
for i in {1..30}    ping -c1 www.google.com &> /dev/null && breakdone
# Run firefox in kiosk mode
firefox --kiosk $KIOSK_URL

Don’t forget to mark the file as runable ( chmod +x ~/.local/bin/kiosk.sh ).

We need to add the kiosk script to the openbox autostart configuration file. For this we create the openbox autostart configuration file directory ( mkdir -p ~/.config/openbox ) and add the path to our kiosk script into the above mentioned file ( echo "$HOME/.local/bin/kiosk.sh" > ~/.config/openbox/autostart ).

Now we can reboot the kiosk and it will automatically start the firefox browser with your grafana playlist.

When your Grafana instance is password protected (which should always be the case) you may need to login on the first reboot so the cookie is placed inside the browser (this can probably be automated). So better find something to distract your flatmate while you borrow their peripherie again for a few minutes.

items.title: Kubernetes Architecture: Understanding Nodes and Processes
items.author: Gudisa Gebi
items.content:
Kubernetes is a powerful orchestration tool that simplifies the management of containers. In this blog, we will explore the basic architecture of Kubernetes, focusing on the different types of nodes and the processes that run on them. This is crucial knowledge for anyone looking to understand how Kubernetes orchestrates applications at scale.

Nodes and Their Processes

Kubernetes clusters consist of , which can be physical or virtual machines. Nodes run the processes that manage the containers, which are packaged as in Kubernetes.

Nodes: The Heart of Kubernetes

There are two primary types of nodes in Kubernetes: and .

  • : These are the machines where the actual work is done. They run the applications (in the form of containers) inside .
  • : These are responsible for controlling and managing the Kubernetes cluster, including the scheduling of pods and maintaining the desired state of the system.

Now, let’s dive into the key processes that run on each node.

A worker node is responsible for running the containers that make up your application. Three essential processes must be installed on every worker node:

  1. The container runtime is the software responsible for running containers on the node. It pulls container images, starts containers, and stops them. Docker is the most widely used container runtime, but alternatives like containerd and CRI-O are also common in Kubernetes.
  2. The Kubelet is an agent that runs on each worker node in the cluster. It ensures that containers are running in the pods. The Kubelet communicates with the master node to retrieve configuration data and reports back the node’s status. It also ensures that the containers are healthy and running as expected.
  3. KubeProxy is responsible for managing network traffic and ensuring that requests are forwarded to the appropriate container within the pod. It maintains network rules and load-balances traffic between the pods, ensuring that requests are always routed to a healthy pod.

Let’s illustrate these processes in a simplified ASCII diagram:

+--------------------------------------------------+|                     Worker Node                  |
|                                                  |
| +-------------------+  +---------------------+   |
| | Container Runtime |  |      Kubelet        |   |
| |                   |  |                     |   |
| |  (Docker, etc.)   |  | - Manages containers |   |
| |                   |  | - Communicates with  |   |
| +-------------------+  |   Master Node        |   |
|                        +---------------------+   |
|                                                  |
| +-----------------+                                |
| |   KubeProxy    |                                |
| |   (Network     |                                |
| |   Traffic)     |                                |
| +-----------------+                                |
+--------------------------------------------------+

This diagram shows the relationship between the processes on a worker node: , , and . They work together to ensure that containers are deployed, running, and networked properly within the cluster.

The master node is responsible for managing and controlling the Kubernetes cluster. It ensures that the cluster runs according to its desired state. Every master node runs four critical processes:

  1. API Server (Cluster Gateway) The API server is the entry point for all interactions with the Kubernetes cluster. It validates and processes requests from users or other components, acting as the for the entire system. It also exposes the Kubernetes API for managing resources.
  2. The scheduler’s job is to decide where the pods should run. Based on available resources and constraints, it assigns pods to specific worker nodes. It works by selecting the best node for each pod according to factors like available CPU, memory, and other constraints.
  3. The controller manager is responsible for monitoring the state of the cluster. It runs various controllers that keep the system in its desired state by detecting changes and taking corrective actions. For example, if a pod goes down, the controller manager will automatically schedule a new pod to replace it.
  4. ETCD is the key-value store that holds the entire state of the Kubernetes cluster. It stores configuration data and the state of all resources, such as pods, nodes, and services. ETCD ensures that Kubernetes can recover the state of the cluster in case of failure or restarts.

Here’s a simple illustration of the master node processes:

+--------------------------------------------------+|                      Master Node                |
|                                                  |
| +-------------------+  +---------------------+   |
| |    API Server     |  |      Scheduler       |   |
| | (Cluster Gateway) |  | - Assigns Pods to    |   |
| | - Validates       |  |   Worker Nodes       |   |
| |   Requests        |  |                     |   |
| +-------------------+  +---------------------+   |
|                                                  |
| +-------------------+  +---------------------+   |
| | Controller        |  |        ETCD         |   |
| | Manager           |  | - Stores Cluster    |   |
| | - Detects Changes |  |   State             |   |
| |   in Cluster      |  |                     |   |
| +-------------------+  +---------------------+   |
+--------------------------------------------------+

How Do We Interact with the Cluster?

Now that we understand the different processes running on the worker and master nodes, let’s look at how users interact with the cluster.

  • : When you want to run a new application, you define a (a logical unit of containers) and request Kubernetes to schedule it. The Kubernetes Scheduler on the master node decides which worker node will run the pod based on available resources and constraints.
  • Monitoring and Managing Pods: The continuously checks the status of the pods and containers running on each worker node. If a container fails or the node becomes unhealthy, the Kubelet can automatically restart the container or re-schedule the pod on another node.
  • : If a pod fails or needs to be rescheduled due to resource constraints, the detects this change and triggers the necessary actions to bring the pod back to a healthy state.

To illustrate a real-world example, let’s imagine a simple Kubernetes cluster with two worker nodes and one master node. The master node runs the API Server, Scheduler, Controller Manager, and ETCD processes, while the worker nodes run the Container Runtime, Kubelet, and KubeProxy.

Here’s how this could look in a cluster:

+----------------------------------+|            Master Node           |
| +----------------------------+  |
| |  API Server (Gateway)      |  |
| |  Scheduler                 |  |
| |  Controller Manager        |  |
| |  ETCD                      |  |
| +----------------------------+  |
+----------------------------------+
            /        \+------------------+   +------------------+
|   Worker Node 1  |   |   Worker Node 2  |
| +--------------+ |   | +--------------+ |  
| | Container   | |   | | Container   | |  
| | Runtime     | |   | | Runtime     | |  
| +--------------+ |   | +--------------+ |  
|   Kubelet       |   |   Kubelet       |   
|   KubeProxy     |   |   KubeProxy     |   
+------------------+   +------------------+
  • The is responsible for managing and controlling the entire cluster.
  • The actually run the containers, with the help of processes like and .

Understanding Kubernetes architecture is foundational for effectively working with this powerful orchestration system. In this blog, we’ve walked through the key components of the and , highlighting the essential processes like , , , and .

By understanding these components and their interactions, you’ll be better equipped to manage and troubleshoot Kubernetes clusters, which is a crucial skill for anyone working in containerized environments.

items.title: Getting Started with AWS S3 and Python (boto3)
items.author: Harikrishnan M
items.content:
When it comes to storing, retrieving, and managing files in the cloud, Amazon Simple Storage Service (S3) is one of the most popular services in the AWS ecosystem. Combined with Python’s versatile library, you can quickly build robust applications that interact with S3 without worrying about the underlying infrastructure.

In this tutorial, you’ll learn how to:

  1. the necessary Python packages and environment variables.
  2. , , and S3 buckets.
  3. , , and files in S3.
  4. Explore multiple  — ranging from image processing to data lakes — that you can integrate into your everyday workflow.

1. Installing the Dependencies

To interact with S3 from Python, install the boto3 library:

Make sure you have the latest version of pip and a set up if you want to keep your dependencies organized.

2. Setting Up Your AWS Credentials

You’ll need AWS credentials with the correct permissions to access S3. Typically, you can store these credentials as environment variables:

    For example, on Linux or macOS, you could export them in your shell:

    export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=YOUR_ACCESS_KEYexport AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=YOUR_SECRET_KEY
    export REGION_NAME=us-east-1

    Alternatively, you can configure them using the AWS CLI with aws configure or store them in ~/.aws/credentials.

    3. Creating the S3 Client

    In your Python code, you’ll import boto3.client and os.getenv to retrieve environment variables:

    from boto3 import clientfrom os import getenv    "s3",
        aws_access_key_id=getenv("AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID"),
        aws_secret_access_key=getenv("AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY"),
        region_name=getenv("REGION_NAME")
    )
    If you have AWS credentials configured via the AWS CLI or environment, you can simply do boto3.client("s3") without manually passing keys.

    Buckets are top-level containers for your data in S3. Let’s explore three common bucket operations: , , and .

    from botocore.exceptions import ClientErrordef create_bucket(bucket_name: str):        response = clientS3.create_bucket(Bucket=bucket_name)    except ClientError as e:
            return e.response["Error"]
    • response = create_bucket("my-awesome-bucket-123") print(response)
    • Note that bucket names across all of AWS (not just within your account).
    def delete_bucket(bucket_name: str):    try:
            response = clientS3.delete_bucket(Bucket=bucket_name)
            return response        return e.response["Error"]
    • response = delete_bucket("my-awesome-bucket-123") print(response)
    def list_buckets():    try:
            response = clientS3.list_buckets()
            return response        return e.response["Error"]
    • response = list_buckets() print(response)
    • The resulting dictionary will include the names of all buckets in your account and their creation dates.

    5. Object (File) Operations

    Now that we can create and manage buckets, it’s time to work with objects — files in S3. Here are three primary operations: , , and .

    from typing import BinaryIOfrom botocore.exceptions import ClientError
    def upload_file(data: BinaryIO, bucket_name: str, filename: str):
        try:
            clientS3.upload_fileobj(Fileobj=data, Bucket=bucket_name, Key=filename)
            return "success"        return e.response["Error"]
    • # Example: uploading a local file with open("local_image.jpg", "rb") as file_data: response = upload_file(file_data, "my-awesome-bucket-123", "images/local_image.jpg") print(response) # Should print "success"
    • This function uses upload_fileobj, perfect for file-like objects, including in-memory buffers.
    def get_file(bucket_name: str, filename: str):    try:
            response = clientS3.get_object(Bucket=bucket_name, Key=filename)
            return response        return e.response["Error"]
    • response = get_file("my-awesome-bucket-123", "images/local_image.jpg") if "Body" in response: file_content = response["Body"].read() # Do something with the file content, like save it locally with open("downloaded_image.jpg", "wb") as f: f.write(file_content)
    def delete_file(bucket_name: str, filename: str):    try:
            response = clientS3.delete_object(Bucket=bucket_name, Key=filename)
            return response        return e.response["Error"]
    • response = delete_file("my-awesome-bucket-123", "images/local_image.jpg") print(response)

    Below are several ways you can integrate these S3 operations into real-world, production-like environments. Feel free to mix and match them based on your project requirements!

    Image Hosting & Processing

    • user-generated images to S3 via your Python back end.
    • Trigger an AWS (via S3 event notifications) to resize, watermark, or convert the image into different formats.
    • Serve optimized images using for global content delivery.
    • Upload daily logs or database snapshots to S3 for secure storage.
    • Retrieve older backups using get_file for quick restores or historical data analysis.
    • Use S3 to move older backups to for cost savings.

    Continuous Integration & Deployment Artifacts

    • Store build artifacts (zipped binaries, Docker images, etc.) in S3 during a CI/CD pipeline.
    • Download those artifacts during deployment to test or production environments.
    • Use object versioning to enable easy rollbacks and maintain historical versions.
    • Host static files (HTML, CSS, JS) in an S3 bucket configured for .
    • Integrate with to map a custom domain name to your S3 website endpoint.
    • Leverage CloudFront for SSL support and caching to improve global performance.

    Log Aggregation & Analytics

    • Consolidate logs from multiple applications or microservices into a single S3 bucket.
    • Use or to query and analyze these logs directly in S3 without loading them into a database.
    • Visualize trends with to gain insights into system performance or user behavior.

    Data Lake & Big Data Processing

    • Treat S3 as a , storing structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data at scale.
    • Trigger AWS Lambda or (Elastic MapReduce) jobs to process large datasets.
    • Integrate with catalogs for schema discovery, making S3 data queryable with SQL-like queries via Amazon Athena.

    Machine Learning Model Storage

    • Store trained ML models (e.g., .pt, .joblib, .h5 files) in S3.
    • Download the models dynamically in your inference script or application using get_file.
    • Keep different model versions in S3 to experiment with A/B testing and easily roll back to older models.

    Large File Uploads with Presigned URLs

    • For front-end applications, generate in Python for direct client-side uploads to S3, reducing server load.
    • Your application remains stateless for file handling, while the S3 bucket manages the data.
    • This setup is especially useful for uploading large media files (videos, high-res images, etc.).
    • : Instead of embedding AWS credentials in your code, assign an IAM role with the necessary S3 permissions to your EC2 instance or container.
    • : S3 bucket policies can restrict who has read/write access. Implement least-privilege permissions.
    • : Enable S3 server-side encryption (SSE) or for sensitive data.
    • : If you need to maintain previous file versions or automatically archive data, enable and on your S3 buckets.
    • : Use to track bucket and object-level API calls, and for metrics like bucket size or request counts.

    By mastering these basic S3 operations — creating buckets, uploading files, retrieving objects, and deleting them — you can build powerful data-driven Python applications on AWS. Whether you’re hosting static content, running data analytics at scale, or automating an image processing pipeline, and together offer a simple and scalable solution.

    • Integrate these functions into a or application for user-driven uploads.
    • Automate data pipelines with and to handle asynchronous tasks.
    • Explore advanced features like , , and to optimize security and cost management.

    If you found this tutorial helpful, feel free to share it, leave a comment, or show some claps. Happy coding!

items.title: Unveiling the Power of Inheritance in Ruby on Rails: Build Smarter, Not Harder
items.author: Rohit Kushwaha
items.content:
Inheritance is one of the cornerstones of object-oriented programming (OOP), and in the world of Ruby on Rails, it’s a superpower that developers can wield to create efficient, maintainable, and scalable applications. Whether you’re working with models, controllers, or custom classes, understanding how inheritance operates in Ruby and Rails can transform the way you structure your codebase. In this deep dive, we’ll explore the nuts and bolts of inheritance in Ruby on Rails, how it ties into the framework’s conventions, and practical examples to help you harness its full potential.

What is Inheritance in Ruby?

At its core, inheritance is an OOP principle that allows one class to inherit properties and behaviors (methods) from another class. Ruby, the language powering Rails, is a pure object-oriented language with single inheritance — meaning a class can inherit from only one parent class (though it can extend functionality using modules, which we’ll touch on later).

In Ruby, inheritance is implemented using the < operator. Here’s a simple example:

class Animal  def speak  end  def bark  endputs dog.speak  # Output: "I make a sound!"
puts dog.bark   # Output: "Woof!"

In this case, Dog inherits from Animal, gaining access to the speak method while adding its own bark method. This is the foundation Rails builds upon.

Inheritance in Ruby on Rails

Rails leverages Ruby’s inheritance model extensively, embedding it into its architecture through the Model-View-Controller (MVC) pattern. Let’s break it down by component:

1. Model Inheritance (Active Record)

In Rails, models inherit from ApplicationRecord, which itself inherits from ActiveRecord::Base. This is where the magic of Rails’ ORM (Object-Relational Mapping) comes into play.

  • : This is the base class for all models in Rails. It provides database interaction methods like save, find, and where.
  • : Introduced in Rails 4.2, this is an abstract base class that sits between your models and ActiveRecord::Base. It’s a convenient place to define shared behavior for all models in your app.

Here’s how it looks in practice:

# app/models/application_record.rbclass ApplicationRecord < ActiveRecord::Base
  self.abstract_class = true
    puts "Created at: #{created_at}"endclass User < ApplicationRecord
  validates :name, presence: true
end
user = User.new(name: "Alice")
user.save
user.log_creation  # Output: "Created at: [timestamp]"

In this example, User inherits from ApplicationRecord, gaining both the log_creation method and all of Active Record’s functionality.

Single Table Inheritance (STI)

Rails takes model inheritance a step further with Single Table Inheritance (STI). STI allows multiple model classes to share a single database table, using a type column to distinguish between them.

# app/models/vehicle.rbclass Vehicle < ApplicationRecordclass Car < Vehicle    "Beep beep!"endclass Truck < Vehicle    "HONK!"end

Database schema (db/schema.rb):

create_table :vehicles do |t|  t.string :type  t.timestamps

When you create a Car or Truck, Rails stores the class name in the type column:

Car.create(name: "Sedan")    # type: "Car"Truck.create(name: "Big Rig") # type: "Truck"

STI is powerful for modeling hierarchical data, but it’s best used when the subclasses share most attributes and differ mainly in behavior.

2. Controller Inheritance

Rails controllers inherit from ApplicationController, which itself inherits from ActionController::Base. This structure lets you define application-wide logic in ApplicationController and override or extend it in specific controllers.

# app/controllers/application_controller.rbclass ApplicationController < ActionController::Base
  before_action :require_login
    redirect_to login_path unless session[:user_id]end
# app/controllers/posts_controller.rb
class PostsController < ApplicationController    @posts = Post.allend

Here, PostsController inherits the require_login filter from ApplicationController. You can override methods or add new ones as needed:

class PostsController < ApplicationController  skip_before_action :require_login, only: :index  # Override inherited filter    @posts = Post.allend

3. Custom Class Inheritance

Beyond models and controllers, you can use inheritance in custom Ruby classes within your Rails app. For instance, let’s create a payment processing system:

# app/services/payment_processor.rbclass PaymentProcessor    raise NotImplementedError, "Subclasses must implement 'process'"end
# app/services/stripe_processor.rb
class StripeProcessor < PaymentProcessor    "Processed $#{amount} via Stripe"end
# app/services/paypal_processor.rb
class PaypalProcessor < PaymentProcessor    "Processed $#{amount} via PayPal"end
stripe = StripeProcessor.newputs stripe.process(50)  # Output: "Processed $50 via Stripe"

This demonstrates polymorphic behavior through inheritance, a pattern often used in Rails for service objects or utilities.

Mixins: Extending Inheritance with Modules

Ruby doesn’t support multiple inheritance, but it offers modules (mixins) as a flexible alternative. Rails uses this extensively with ActiveSupport::Concern. For example:

# app/models/concerns/taggable.rbmodule Taggable
  extend ActiveSupport::Concern
    has_many :tags    tags.pluck(:name).join(", ")endclass Post < ApplicationRecordend

Now, Post inherits the tags association and tag_list method via the Taggable module.

Pros and Cons of Inheritance in Rails

  • Reduces code duplication (DRY principle).
  • Encourages modular, reusable design.
  • Aligns with Rails’ convention-over-configuration philosophy.
  • Overuse of STI can lead to bloated tables and confusion.
  • Deep inheritance hierarchies can become hard to maintain.
  • Tight coupling between parent and child classes can reduce flexibility.
  1. : Prefer composition (e.g., modules) or separate tables unless the hierarchy is simple and stable.
  2. Leverage ApplicationController: Centralize shared controller logic here, but keep it lean.
  3. : Use ApplicationRecord or custom base classes for shared model functionality.
  4. : Inheritance can introduce subtle bugs — cover edge cases in your test suite.

Inheritance in Ruby on Rails is a double-edged sword: wielded wisely, it simplifies your code and amplifies productivity; used recklessly, it can complicate your app’s architecture. By mastering model inheritance, controller hierarchies, and custom class structures — while supplementing with mixins — you’ll unlock the full power of Rails’ OOP capabilities. So go forth, build smarter, and let inheritance do the heavy lifting for you!

items.title: Shift-Left Observability: Visualizing k6 Load Tests with Prometheus & Grafana
items.author: Sumit Soman
items.content:
Observability is a key pillar of performance testing, ensuring that you can monitor, analyze, and optimize system behavior in real time. In this blog, we take the approach further by integrating k6, Prometheus, and Grafana, enabling real-time visualization of load test metrics.This is a continuation of our previous blog, “Shift-Left Performance Testing: Integrating k6 in CI/CD Pipelines,” where we performed API load testing using k6. Now, we will:

  • Create k6 script to performance Load Test on crocodile Get api.
  • Created a CI/CD pipeline for continuous load test on each code push.

Step 1: Set Up Grafana Cloud Prometheus

  1. Navigate to “Connections” → “Prometheus” in the Grafana Cloud Portal.
  2. Copy the Remote Write URL, username, and API key (you’ll need this for k6).

Step 2: Configure k6 to Push Metrics to Grafana Cloud Prometheus

We will modify our to send results directly to .

Modify the k6 Test Script

import http from 'k6/http';import { check, sleep } from 'k6';export let options = {    { duration: '10s', target: 10 }, // Ramp up to 50 users in 30s
    { duration: '10s', target: 10 },  // Stay at 50 users for 1m
    { duration: '10s', target: 0 }   // Ramp down to 0 users  ext: {      projectID: 12345, // Replace with your project ID if needed    output: ["prometheus_remote_write"],};
export default function () {
  let res = http.get('https://test-api.k6.io/public/crocodiles/');    'response status is 200': (r) => r.status === 200,
    'response time is < 500ms': (r) => r.timings.duration < 500,
  });}

Run the k6 Test with Grafana Cloud Prometheus

Replace <YOUR_REMOTE_WRITE_URL> and <YOUR_USERNAME> with the credentials from :

K6_PROMETHEUS_REMOTE_WRITE_URL=<YOUR_REMOTE_WRITE_URL> \K6_PROMETHEUS_REMOTE_WRITE_USERNAME=<YOUR_USERNAME> \
K6_PROMETHEUS_REMOTE_WRITE_PASSWORD=<YOUR_API_KEY> \
k6 cloud run load_test.js

Navigate to the url to see the results. It used the k6 cloud resources to run the load tests. Onces the execution is finish, it publishes the result which we can visualize as Dashboards as per user requirement.

Step 3: Set Up Grafana Cloud Dashboard

  1. Navigate to “Dashboards” → “New Dashboard”
  2. Add Prometheus as a Data Source
  3. Enter the from Grafana Cloud.

We can visualize all metric from checks, Https, logs, traffic etc on the dashboard.

We successfully integrated k6 with Grafana Cloud Prometheus and visualized in . This enables real-time observability, helping teams quickly and optimize system performance.

ensures that performance insights are in the development cycle.

items.title: Spring Boot Performance Monitoring: Visualize HTTP Latency & Errors (Micrometer + Grafana)
items.author: Kaan Atesel
items.content:
In modern web applications, performance and reliability are crucial. Even small delays impact user experience and business revenue. Without proper monitoring, slow endpoints, API failures, and performance regressions go undetected.

Beyond tracking slow requests, it’s essential to understand why and when performance degrades — due to traffic spikes, database bottlenecks, memory leaks, or inefficient code. By monitoring HTTP request duration and response status codes, we can analyze trends, detect slowdowns, and pinpoint issues.

One key metric in is , which helps identify . Tracking response status codes (e.g., 200 OK, 404 Not Found) allows developers to spot error trends and API failures.

This guide will walk you through setting up effective monitoring in a , which you can apply to any existing project.

If you want to use demo project you can jump to the Setting Up a Demo Project section.

Exporting Metrics in a Spring Boot Application Using Actuator

Spring Boot provides built-in monitoring and metrics collection through . By enabling Actuator and integrating , the application can export metrics to , which allows real-time monitoring of request duration, response statuses, memory usage, and other system health indicators. Actuator exposes an endpoint (/actuator/prometheus) that provides structured metrics, which Prometheus can scrape and store for further analysis.

<dependency>   <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
   <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-actuator</artifactId>
</dependency>   <groupId>io.micrometer</groupId>
   <artifactId>micrometer-registry-prometheus</artifactId>
</dependency>
...management:    web:        include: "*"      enabled: true    web:        request:            enabled: true      percentiles-histogram:
        http.server.requests: true
      percentiles:
        http.server.requests: 0.5,0.95,0.99
      slo:
        http.server.requests: 100ms,250ms,500ms,1s,5s
    tags:...

After that you can run your application and observe that the metrics are exported and collected.

Check that all metrics are accessible by Prometheus.

Creating a New Dashboard in Grafana to Track HTTP Requests

To monitor HTTP request duration, response status codes, and error rates from the Spring Boot application, a can be created. This will provide insights into API performance, identify slow endpoints, and detect potential issues.

However, instead of manually configuring everything, Grafana also provides community created dashboards specifically designed for Spring Boot Actuator and Micrometer metrics. These dashboards offer detailed insights into request performance, JVM memory usage, system health, and more — without additional setup.

Import Spring Boot Actuator Dashboards

For , use a community created Grafana dashboard designed for Spring Boot Actuator metrics.

1️⃣ Spring Boot 2.1 Statistics Dashboard (ID: 10280)

  • Covers request counts, durations, status codes, and JVM metrics.
  • Grafana Dashboard 10280

2️⃣ Micrometer JVM Dashboard (ID: 4701)

  • Focuses on JVM memory, GC, threads, and performance.
  • Grafana Dashboard 4701

3️⃣ Micrometer Spring Boot Dashboard (ID: 11378)

  • Includes web request metrics, memory usage, and database stats.
  • Grafana Dashboard 11378

Create a new data source:

  1. Click data source from the left menu
  2. Click Add new data source
  3. Select Prometheus and fill required fields

Note: You can use Prometheus as a domain name because docker compose creates its own DNS server so you can access the other services.

  1. Click Dashboards on the left menu
  2. Then click new and import
  3. Use the ID of the dashboard and select the correct data source

Your dashboard should be ready.

Creating a Dashboard in Grafana to Track HTTP Requests

You can also create a custom dashboard using PromQL queries and select the appropriate visualization to effectively monitor your metrics.

Visualization Type: Stat Panel

up{job="spring-boot-app"}

2. Average Response Time per Endpoint

Visualization Type: Bar Gauge

up{job="spring-boot-app"}

Visualization Type: Time Series

sum(rate(http_server_requests_seconds_count[1m]))

Visualization Type: Gauge

(sum(rate(http_server_requests_seconds_count{status=~"2.."}[1m])) / sum(rate(http_server_requests_seconds_count[1m]))) * 100

Visualization Type: Bar Gauge

sum(rate(http_server_requests_seconds_count{status=~"4.."}[1m])) by (uri, method)

6. Total Error Rate (4xx + 5xx)

Visualization Type: Bar Gauge

sum(rate(http_server_requests_seconds_count{status=~"4..|5.."}[1m])) by (uri, method)

7. Request Duration (Average Response Time)

Visualization Type: Time Series

sum(rate(http_server_requests_seconds_sum[1m])) by (uri, method) /sum(rate(http_server_requests_seconds_count[1m])) by (uri, method)

Setting Up a Demo Project

Next, I will create a controller that randomly returns success or failure responses at varying response times.

package com.example.demo;
import org.springframework.http.HttpStatus;
import org.springframework.http.ResponseEntity;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.GetMapping;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.RequestMapping;
import org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.RestController;
import java.util.concurrent.TimeUnit;@RequestMapping("/api")
public class RandomResponseController {
   private final Random random = new Random();
    * Generates a random delay between min and max seconds.   private void randomDelay(int minSeconds, int maxSeconds) {
       int delay = random.nextInt(maxSeconds - minSeconds + 1) + minSeconds;
       try {
           TimeUnit.SECONDS.sleep(delay);
       } catch (InterruptedException e) {
           Thread.currentThread().interrupt();
       }    * Randomly returns HTTP status codes:
    * - 80% chance of 2xx (OK)
    * - 10% chance of 4xx (Client Error)
    * - 10% chance of 5xx (Server Error)
    */
   private HttpStatus getRandomStatus() {
       int chance = random.nextInt(100); // Generate a number between 0-99           return HttpStatus.OK; // 80% success
       } else if (chance < 90) {
           return HttpStatus.BAD_REQUEST; // 10% 4xx error           return HttpStatus.INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR; // 10% 5xx error   }   public ResponseEntity<String> fastEndpoint() {       return new ResponseEntity<>("Fast Response", getRandomStatus());   public ResponseEntity<String> mediumEndpoint() {       return new ResponseEntity<>("Medium Response", getRandomStatus());   public ResponseEntity<String> slowEndpoint() {       return new ResponseEntity<>("Slow Response", getRandomStatus());
   @GetMapping("/very-slow")
   public ResponseEntity<String> verySlowEndpoint() {       return new ResponseEntity<>("Very Slow Response", getRandomStatus());}

This is a python script so that you can send requests and tests that are working. You can run several times so you can mimic that the application has a load.

import requestsimport random
# Base URL of the Spring Boot application
BASE_URL = "http://localhost:8080/api"ENDPOINTS = ["/fast", "/medium", "/slow", "/very-slow"]
# Function to send a request to a randomly selected endpoint
def send_request():
   endpoint = random.choice(ENDPOINTS)
   url = BASE_URL + endpoint       start_time = time.time()  # Start time tracking
       response = requests.get(url, timeout=25)  # Send GET request with a timeout
       end_time = time.time()  # End time tracking
       response_time = round(end_time - start_time, 2)  # Calculate response time
       print(f"Request to {endpoint} -> Status: {response.status_code}, Time: {response_time}s")
   except requests.exceptions.Timeout:
       print(f"Request to {endpoint} -> Timed out!")
   except requests.exceptions.RequestException as e:
       print(f"Request to {endpoint} -> Failed! Error: {e}")
# Continuously send requests at random intervals
while True:

Deploying Prometheus and Grafana with Docker for Metrics Visualization

Now that the Spring Boot application is collecting metrics, the next step is to deploy Prometheus and Grafana to store, query, and visualize these metrics. I will use Docker to simplify deployment and ensure consistency across environments. Prometheus will act as the time-series database, scraping metrics from the Spring Boot application’s /actuator/prometheus endpoint, while Grafana will provide a powerful and flexible UI to analyze and visualize the collected data.I will use , making the deployment process quick and consistent across environments.

First dockerize the demo application:

FROM openjdk:17-jdk-slimWORKDIR /app
COPY target/simple-web-service-0.0.1-SNAPSHOT.jar app.jar
EXPOSE 8080
ENTRYPOINT ["java", "-jar", "/app/app.jar"]

The Prometheus need to configured to collect metrics from the demo application.

global: scrape_interval: 5s
scrape_configs: - job_name: 'spring-boot-app'     - targets: ['spring-boot-app:8080']
   metrics_path: /actuator/prometheus

And create a docker-compose yaml to deploy all of the apps with ease.

version: '3.7'services:   build:     dockerfile: Dockerfile
   container_name: spring-boot-app
   ports:   networks: prometheus:
   image: prom/prometheus:latest
   container_name: prometheus     - ./prometheus.yml:/etc/prometheus/prometheus.yml     - "9090:9090"     - monitoring   image: grafana/grafana:latest   depends_on:   ports:   environment:
     - GF_SECURITY_ADMIN_PASSWORD=admin
   volumes:
     - grafana_data:/var/lib/grafana
   networks:volumes:networks:

create new image for the application

Observed that all the containers are running

Go to Prometheus UI (http://localhost:9090/query0 ) and see that metrics are collected

go to the grafana dashboard and login

items.title: Distributed Transactions in Microservices: An Introduction to the Saga Pattern
items.author: Ahmed Adel
items.content:
One of the biggest challenges in apps with microservices architecture is how we can manage distributed transactions across multiple services.

What will you learn in this article?

  • What are distributed transactions?
  • What is the problem that appears during managing distributed transactions?
  • What is the saga pattern, and how can it solve the problem?
  • How to implement the saga pattern?

Before diving deep into the saga pattern, there are some theoretical concepts that must be well known. If you are aware of these concepts, you can skip this section.

What are ACID transactions?

A transaction is a group of operations (queries) that must be treated as one unit. And if one query fails, the whole transaction must fail, and all transactions must be rolled back.

If there is a transferring of money from account A to account B.

1. Subtract the amount of money from account A.

2. Adding the amount to account B.

In this example the two operations must be executed as one operation. And there are properties that must exist in the transaction to achieve consistency and integrity of data.

all operations inside a transaction must be successful or failed (all or nothing). If one query fails, the transaction must be rolled back. In the above example, if adding the amount to account B fails, the subtracted amount from account A must be returned to account A by rolling back the transaction.

the data must be consistent before and after the transaction, and there must be no violation of database constraints. In the above example, if there is a constraint on the money column (must be a positive number), after the transaction, the money column must not contain a negative number.

ensures that even though transactions are executed concurrently, their effects are as if they were executed serially.

once the transaction is committed, the data must be persistent on disk.

What are compensating transactions?

Compensating transactions are transactions that reverse (undo) effect of another transaction.

For example if there is transaction that decrease amount of money the compensating transaction of it is transaction that increase this amount of money.

What are distributed transactions?

In microservices architecture applications, each service has its own database, and some user’s actions require accessing multiple services, and these actions must run as one transaction. So the transactions that need access data in different databases in different networks are called distributed transactions.

In an e-commerce app, a customer needs to make an order.

The steps to do that are:

2. Decrease the customer credit

And these steps must be run as one transaction.

If this app is a monolithic architecture app, creating a transaction that contains these steps is very easy due to all this data in one database.

If this app is a microservice architecture app and there are three separated databases, one for orders, one for customers, and one for inventory, so creating one local transaction that guarantee ACID Properties is not applicable. So we need something to manage this transaction, and there are many patterns, like the 2-phase commit and saga pattern.

The saga pattern manages distributed transactions by dividing the distributed transaction into small local transactions in each service, and when one transaction succeeds, it publishes an event to the next service to start the next local transaction and so on till all local transactions are successful, and if one local transaction fails, there are compensating transactions that will run to reverse the committed local transactions (undo the successful transaction). The purpose of this compensating transaction is to make the data consistent.

How to implement saga pattern?

There are two options for implantation:

In this option, each service is responsible for notifying the next service to start its transaction till all local transactions are succeeded, and if one local transaction fails, this service is responsible for running its compensating transaction and publishing an event to notify the next service to run its compensating transaction and so on till all compensating transactions are succeeded and the data becomes consistent.

In our example of E-commerce app

1. Client calls API to make an order, and the request is received by the order service.

2. The order service Start a local transaction to create an order, then publish an event order_created to customer service and end the local transaction.

3. The customer service Receive order_created and start its local transaction and check client credit and decrease it, then publish an event order_paid to the inventory service, then end its local transaction.

4. The order is paid, and the inventory service starts its local transaction and checks product quantity and decreases its quantities, then ends the transaction. And now the order is completed successfully, and the databases are in a consistent state.

1. Client calls API to make an order, and the request is received by the order service.

2. The order service Start a local transaction to create an order, then publish an event order_created to customer service and end the local transaction.

3. The customer service Receive order_created and start its local transaction and check client credit, but the customer’s credit is not enough to complete this order, so the transaction will fail, and the customer service will run its compensating transaction and then publish an event order_canceled to the order service.

4. The order_canceled is received by the order service, and it starts its compensating transaction. And now the order is not completed, and the databases are in a consistent state.

In this option there is an orchestrator service responsible for starting each local transaction and starting compensating transactions in the failure case (managing transaction flow is centralized in the orchestrator service).

In our example of E-commerce app

1. The client calls the API to make an order, and the request is received by the orchestrator service, which publishes an event to the order service to start its local transaction.

2. The order service Start a local transaction to create an order, then publish an event order_created to the orchestrator service and end the local transaction.

3. The orchestrator service Receive order_created and publish the event to customer service; then the customer service receives order_created and starts its local transaction and checks client credit and decreases it, then publishes an event order_paid to the orchestrator service, then ends its local transaction.

4. The orchestrator service Receive order_paid and publish the event to the inventory service. Then the order_paid is received by the inventory service, and it starts its local transaction and checks product quantity and decreases its quantities, then ends the transaction, then publishes an event to the orchestrator service to notify it the transaction is succeeded. And now the order is completed successfully, and the databases are in a consistent state.

1. The client calls the API to make an order, and the request is received by the orchestrator service, which publishes an event to the order service to start its local transaction.

2. The order service Start a local transaction to create an order, then publish an event order_created to the orchestrator service and end the local transaction.

3. The orchestrator service Receive order_created and publish the event to customer service; then the customer service receives order_created and starts its local transaction and checks client credit, but the customer’s credit is not enough to complete this order, so the transaction will fail, and the customer service will publish ordered_canceled to the orchestrator service.

4. The order_canceled is received by the orchestrator service and publishes the event to customer service to start its compensating transaction, then the customer service publishes an event to the orchestrator, and the orchestrator publishes an event to the order service to start its compensating transaction. And now the order is not completed, and the databases are in a consistent state.

Use Choreography based saga or Orchestration based saga ?

Choreography-based is suitable for simple distributed transactions and when you need to avoid single point of failure, but orchestration-based can handle more complex distributed transactions but this orchestrator service may be single point of failure in your application and the scalability and availability may be effected, so it depends on the application and the number of services and transaction complexity.

Before starting to use the saga pattern, there are some points that must be considered.

1. The saga pattern does not support ACID properties for transactions. According to asynchronous updates between services, there is no isolation.

2. The saga pattern supports only eventual consistency due to asynchronous updates; some data may not be updated versions.

3. The usage of saga pattern may affect the application performance due to asynchronous communication and extra service like orchestrator service.

4. The retiring and events delivery grantees must be considered to grantee data consistency.
