items.title: Anthropic’s Claude 3.7 Sonnet hybrid reasoning model is now available in Amazon Bedrock
items.author: Esra Kayabali
items.content:
Amazon Bedrock is expanding its foundation model (FM) offerings as the generative AI field evolves. Today, we’re excited to announce the availability of Anthropic’s Claude 3.7 Sonnet foundation model in Amazon Bedrock. As Anthropic’s most intelligent model to date, Claude 3.7 Sonnet stands out as their first hybrid reasoning model capable of producing quick responses or , meaning it can work through difficult problems using careful, step-by-step reasoning. Additionally, today we are adding Claude 3.7 Sonnet to the list of models used by Amazon Q Developer. Amazon Q is built on Bedrock, and with Amazon Q you can use the most appropriate model for a specific task such as Claude 3.7 Sonnet, for more advanced coding workflows that enable developers to accelerate building across the entire software development lifecycle.

Here are several notable features and capabilities of Claude 3.7 Sonnet in Amazon Bedrock.

The first Claude model with hybrid reasoning – Claude 3.7 Sonnet takes a different approach to how models think. Instead of using separate models—one for quick answers and another for solving complex problems—Claude 3.7 Sonnet integrates reasoning as a core capability within a single model. This combination is more similar to how the human brains works. After all, we use the same brain whether we’re answering a simple question or solving a difficult puzzle.

The model has two modes— and mode—which can be toggled in Amazon Bedrock. In mode, Claude 3.7 Sonnet is an improved version of Claude 3.5 Sonnet. In mode, Claude 3.7 Sonnet takes additional time to analyze problems in detail, plan solutions, and consider multiple perspectives before providing a response, allowing it to make further gains in performance. You can control speed and cost by choosing when to use reasoning capabilities. Extended thinking tokens count towards the context window and are billed as output tokens.

Anthropic’s most powerful model for coding – Claude 3.7 Sonnet is state-of-the art for coding, excelling in understanding context and creative problem solving, and according to Anthropic, achieves an industry-leading 70.3% for mode on SWE-bench Verified. Claude 3.7 Sonnet also performs better than Claude 3.5 Sonnet across the majority of benchmarks. These enhanced capabilities make Claude 3.7 Sonnet ideal for powering AI agents and complex workflows.

Over 15x longer output capacity than its predecessor – Compared to Claude 3.5 Sonnet, this model offers significantly expanded output length. This enhanced capacity is particularly useful when you explicitly request more detail, ask for multiple examples, or request additional context or background information. To achieve long outputs, try asking for a detailed outline (for writing use cases, you can specify outline detail down to the paragraph level and include word count targets). Then, ask for the response to index its paragraphs to the outline and reiterate the word counts. Claude 3.7 Sonnet supports outputs up to 128K tokens long (up to 64K as generally available and up to 128K as a beta).

Adjustable reasoning budget – You can control the budget for thinking when you use Claude 3.7 Sonnet in Amazon Bedrock. This flexibility helps you weigh the trade-offs between speed, cost, and performance. By allocating more tokens to reasoning for complex problems or limiting tokens for faster responses, you can optimize performance for your specific use case.

As for any new model, I have to request access in the Amazon Bedrock console. In the navigation pane, I choose under Bedrock configurations. Then, I choose to request access for Claude 3.7 Sonnet.

To try Claude 3.7 Sonnet, I choose  under  in the navigation pane. Then I choose  and choose  under the and  under the . To enable the mode, I toggle under . I type the following prompt, and choose :

You're the manager of a small restaurant facing these challenges:

Three staff members called in sick for tonight's dinner service
You're expecting a full house (80 seats)
There's a large party of 20 coming at 7 PM
Your main chef is available but two kitchen helpers are among those who called in sick
You have 2 regular servers and 1 trainee available
How would you:

Reorganize the available staff to handle the situation
Prioritize tasks and service
Determine if you need to make any adjustments to reservations
Handle the large party while maintaining service quality
Minimize negative impact on customer experience
Explain your reasoning for each decision and discuss potential trade-offs

Here’s the result with an animated image showing the reasoning process of the model.

To test image-to-text vision capabilities, I upload an image of a detailed architectural site plan created using Amazon Bedrock. I receive a detailed analysis and reasoned insights of this site plan.

Claude 3.7 Sonnet’s enhanced capabilities can benefit multiple industry use cases. Businesses can create advanced AI assistants and agents that interact directly with customers. In fields such as healthcare, it can assist in medical imaging analysis and research summarization, and financial services can benefit from its abilities to solve complex financial modeling problems. For developers, it serves as a coding companion that can review code, explain technical concepts, and suggest improvements across different languages.

Anthropic’s Claude 3.7 Sonnet is available today in the US East (N. Virginia), US East (Ohio), and US West (Oregon) Regions. Check the full Region list for future updates.

Claude 3.7 Sonnet is priced competitively and matches the price of Claude 3.5 Sonnet. For pricing details, refer to the Amazon Bedrock pricing page.

— Esra

items.title: How to Automatically Restart Docker Containers After Reboots (EC2 & Linux Servers)
items.author: Jaecom
items.content:
Learn how to automate Docker container restarts on AWS EC2 or Linux servers using systemd. No more manual reboots — get your containers up in seconds!

Manually restarting Docker containers after every EC2 reboot can be frustrating. By using systemd, you can automatically run a custom script that brings containers back up each time the server restarts. This guide will show you how to:

  • Create a shell script to manage container restarts.
  • Configure a systemd service to execute the script.
  • Enable and test the service so it runs on every reboot.

start-containers.sh Script

First, you’ll need a shell script that stops and removes old containers, then spins up fresh Docker containers every time it’s run. This script will later be used by our service to handle container restarts on every reboot.

Create a directory for the script:

cd ~mkdir reboot-script
vim reboot-script/start-containers.sh

Create and edit the script to stop, remove & start your Docker containers:

#!/bin/bash
# Stop and remove old containers if they exist
docker container stop nginx-container campable-server || true
docker container rm nginx-container campable-server || true
docker run -p 8000:8000 -d --name campable-server --network campable-network campable-server
# Using Docker Compose for my Nginx container
docker compose -f /home/ubuntu/nginx/nginx-compose.yml up -d

docker-reboot-containers.service File

A systemdservice tells the operating system exactly how and when to run a process. Here, it will execute the start-containers.sh script after Docker is ready.

Create the service file with root privileges:

sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/docker-reboot-containers.service

Add the following configuration:

[Unit]Description=Docker Container RestartAfter=docker.service# The file path of your start-containers.sh
ExecStart=/bin/bash /home/ubuntu/reboot-script/start-containers.sh
# Automatically restart if service fails
Restart=on-failure
# How long to wait before attempting another restart
RestartSec=10
# Limit the number of restarts in a given interval
StartLimitBurst=5WantedBy=multi-user.target
  • Requires=docker.service ensures Docker is running before the script starts.
  • Restart=on-failure tells systemd service to restart only when the process exits with a non-zero exit code, which typically indicates a failure.
  • StartLimitBurst=5 and StartLimitIntervalSec=70 prevent excessive restart attempts by exiting the restart if the number of restarts exceeds 5 times in 70 seconds.

Step 3: Enable and Start the Systemd Service

Now that the service file is ready, we need to have systemd recognize the service and enable it to run every time the system boots.

The service file is located at /etc/systemd/system/docker-reboot-containers.service. When enabling the service, automatically searches for /etc/systemd/system directory (and other standard directories, such as /lib/systemd/system). This is important because it ensures that your service file is properly registered and linked within the

Enable the docker-reboot-containers service:

# Reload systemd configsudo systemctl daemon-reloadsudo systemctl enable docker-reboot-containers

You should see output like this:

Created symlink /etc/systemd/system/multi-user.target.wants/docker-reboot-containers.service → /etc/systemd/system/docker-reboot-containers.service.

This confirms that systemd has created a symlink in the multi-user.target.wants directory, pointing to your service file, and the service is now set to run on system boot.

sudo systemctl start docker-reboot-containers
● docker-reboot-containers.service - Docker Container Restart     Loaded: loaded (/etc/systemd/system/docker-reboot-containers.service; enabled; preset: enabled)
     Active: active (running) since Sun 2025-01-12 12:58:53 UTC; 8s ago
   Main PID: 26123 (bash)     Memory: 7.2M (peak: 7.5M)     CGroup: /system.slice/docker-reboot-containers.service
             ├─26123 /bin/bash /home/ubuntu/reboot-script/start-containers.sh
             └─26130 docker container stop nginx-container web-server

It seems to have successfully executed the script! To double check, list the the docker processes to see if the containers have been deleted and recreated:

sudo docker ps
# Created 6 seconds ago, the service is working!
CONTAINER ID   IMAGE             COMMAND                  CREATED         STATUS         PORTS                                                                      NAMES
680717d82eea   nginx:latest      "/docker-entrypoint.…"   6 seconds ago   Up 5 seconds   0.0.0.0:80->80/tcp, :::80->80/tcp, 0.0.0.0:443->443/tcp, :::443->443/tcp   nginx-container
14ed6d48b07b   web-server        "gunicorn --bind 0.0…"   6 seconds ago   Up 6 seconds   0.0.0.0:8000->8000/tcp, :::8000->8000/tcp                                  web-server

The docker containers were created 6 seconds ago, confirming that our docker-reboot-containers service has successfully run.

Step 4: Confirm at Reboot

Now, let’s reboot our EC2 instance and check if the service executes our script automatically.

Perform a reboot of your EC2 Container:

SSH into your EC Container, and check the Docker processes again:

sudo docker ps
# Created 32 seconds ago, the service is working!
CONTAINER ID   IMAGE             COMMAND                  CREATED         STATUS         PORTS                                                                      NAMES
680717d82eea   nginx:latest      "/docker-entrypoint.…"   32 seconds ago   Up 30 seconds   0.0.0.0:80->80/tcp, :::80->80/tcp, 0.0.0.0:443->443/tcp, :::443->443/tcp   nginx-container
14ed6d48b07b   web-server        "gunicorn --bind 0.0…"   32 seconds ago   Up 30 seconds   0.0.0.0:8000->8000/tcp, :::8000->8000/tcp                                  web-server

With the Docker containers created 32 seconds ago, the service seems to be successfully working.

By creating a simple script and tying it to a systemd service, we can eliminate the need to manually restart our Docker containers on every reboot. This approach saves time, reduces human error, and ensures our services are always up and running quickly. Happy automating!

items.title: Kubernetes the Hard Way on Raspberry Pi , Docker & EC2 (Part 4): Control Plane, Worker Nodes &…
items.author: Jaecom
items.content:
Kubernetes the Hard Way on Raspberry Pi , Docker & EC2 (Part 4): Control Plane, Worker Nodes & Testing

Welcome to , the final installment of our Kubernetes the Hard Way series on ! At this point, we have:

  • A via WireGuard VPN
  • and for Kubernetes components
  • A key-value store

Now, we’ll bring it all together by:

  • Deploying the
  • Bringing worker nodes fully online
  • Configuring pod networking
  • Running a (including a sample Nginx deployment)

1. Bootstrapping the Kubernetes Control Plane

The control plane consists of the , , and . We’ll copy the necessary binaries and systemd unit files to the server container, configure them, and then start the services.

On your jumpbox container, copy the Kubernetes binaries and systemd unit files to the server container:

scp \  downloads-amd/kube-apiserver \
  downloads-amd/kube-controller-manager \
  downloads-amd/kube-scheduler \  units/kube-apiserver.service \
  units/kube-controller-manager.service \
  units/kube-scheduler.service \
  configs/kube-scheduler.yaml \
  configs/kube-apiserver-to-kubelet.yaml \

1.2 Install kubernetes controller binaries

mkdir -p /etc/kubernetes/config  chmod +x kube-apiserver \
    kube-controller-manager \
    kube-scheduler kubectl    kube-controller-manager \    /usr/local/bin/

1.3 Configure the following: kube-apiserver, kube-controller-manager, kube-scheduler:

mkdir -p /var/lib/kubernetes/   kube-api-server.key kube-api-server.crt \
   service-accounts.key service-accounts.crt \
   encryption-config.yaml \

Move the systemd unit files and configurations:

mv kube-apiserver.service \  /etc/systemd/system/kube-apiserver.service
mv kube-controller-manager.kubeconfig /var/lib/kubernetes/
mv kube-controller-manager.service /etc/systemd/system/
mv kube-scheduler.kubeconfig /var/lib/kubernetes/
mv kube-scheduler.yaml /etc/kubernetes/config/
mv kube-scheduler.service /etc/systemd/system/

1.4 Start the Controller Services

systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl enable kube-apiserver \
  kube-controller-manager kube-scheduler
systemctl start kube-apiserver \
  kube-controller-manager kube-scheduler
kubectl cluster-info \  --kubeconfig admin.kubeconfig

You should see something like:

Kubernetes control plane is running at https://127.0.0.1:6443

Check the status for any errors:

systemctl status kube-apiserver kube-controller-manager kube-scheduler

You might see errors related to cluster role permissions; we’ll fix those next.

1.5 RBAC for Kubelet Authorization

Create the system:kube-apiserver-to-kubelet ClusterRole with permissions to access the Kubelet API:

kubectl apply -f kube-apiserver-to-kubelet.yaml \  --kubeconfig admin.kubeconfig

Restart the controller services to apply the role permissions:

  systemctl daemon-reload
  systemctl restart kube-apiserver \
    kube-controller-manager kube-scheduler

Check the staus of kube-apiserver for any errors:

systemctl status kube-apiserver kube-controller-manager kube-scheduler

1.6 API Server Verification

From the jumpbox, verify the API server is reachable:

curl -k --cacert ca.crt https://server.kubernetes.local:6443/version

You should see JSON output with version details:

{  "major": "1",  "gitVersion": "v1.32.0",
  "gitCommit": "70d3cc986aa8221cd1dfb1121852688902d3bf53",
  "gitTreeState": "clean",
  "buildDate": "2024-12-11T17:59:15Z",
  "goVersion": "go1.23.3",  "platform": "linux/amd64"

2. Bootstrapping the Kubernetes Worker Nodes

Next, we’ll install the , , and on each worker node: node-0 (EC2 B) and node-1(Raspberry Pi).

From the jumpbox, copy the correct binaries and configs to each worker node:

for host in node-0 node-1; do  SUBNET=$(grep $host machines.txt | cut -d " " -f 4)
  sed "s|SUBNET|$SUBNET|g" \
    configs/10-bridge.conf > 10-bridge.conf
  sed "s|SUBNET|$SUBNET|g" \
    configs/kubelet-config.yaml > kubelet-config.yaml
  scp 10-bridge.conf kubelet-config.yaml \
  root@$host:~/

Then copy the rest of the binaries and config files appropriately to node-0 (AMD64) and node-1 (ARM64):

# Node-0 amd packagesscp \
  downloads-amd/runc.amd64 \
  downloads-amd/crictl-v1.32.0-linux-amd64.tar.gz \
  downloads-amd/cni-plugins-linux-amd64-v1.6.0.tgz \
  downloads-amd/containerd-2.0.0-linux-amd64.tar.gz \  downloads-amd/kubelet \
  downloads-amd/kube-proxy \
  configs/99-loopback.conf \
  configs/containerd-config.toml \
  configs/kube-proxy-config.yaml \
  units/containerd.service \
  units/kubelet.service \
  units/kube-proxy.service \
  root@node-0:~/

Raspberry Pi Node-1 (AMD64):

# Node-1 arm packagesscp \
  downloads-arm/runc.arm64 \
  downloads-arm/crictl-v1.32.0-linux-arm64.tar.gz \
  downloads-arm/cni-plugins-linux-arm64-v1.6.0.tgz \
  downloads-arm/containerd-2.0.0-linux-arm64.tar.gz \  downloads-arm/kubelet \
  downloads-arm/kube-proxy \
  configs/99-loopback.conf \
  configs/containerd-config.toml \
  configs/kube-proxy-config.yaml \
  units/containerd.service \
  units/kubelet.service \
  units/kube-proxy.service \
  root@node-1:~/
From now on, the commands in this lab must be run on each worker instance: node-0, node-1.

SSH into each node to proceed:

ssh root@node-0ssh root@node-1

2.2 Provisioning a Kubernetes Worker Node

{  apt update
  apt -y install socat conntrack ipset kmod # Install kmod for overlay
}

Check if swap is disabled:

No output means swap is disabled. Otherwise, refer to Part 1 to disable swap.

2.4 Create the installation directories

mkdir -p \  /etc/cni/net.d \  /var/lib/kubelet \  /var/lib/kubernetes \  mkdir -p containerd
  tar -xvf crictl-v1.32.0-linux-amd64.tar.gz
  tar -xvf containerd-2.0.0-linux-amd64.tar.gz -C containerd
  tar -xvf cni-plugins-linux-amd64-v1.6.0.tgz -C /opt/cni/bin/
  mv runc.amd64 runc
  chmod +x crictl kubectl kube-proxy kubelet runc
  mv crictl kubectl kube-proxy kubelet runc /usr/local/bin/
  mv containerd/bin/* /bin/
}

Raspberry Pi Node-1 (AMD64):

mkdir -p \  /etc/cni/net.d \  /var/lib/kubelet \  /var/lib/kubernetes \  mkdir -p containerd
  tar -xvf crictl-v1.32.0-linux-arm64.tar.gz
  tar -xvf containerd-2.0.0-linux-arm64.tar.gz -C containerd
  tar -xvf cni-plugins-linux-arm64-v1.6.0.tgz -C /opt/cni/bin/
  mv runc.arm64 runc
  chmod +x crictl kubectl kube-proxy kubelet runc
  mv crictl kubectl kube-proxy kubelet runc /usr/local/bin/
  mv containerd/bin/* /bin/
}
mv 10-bridge.conf 99-loopback.conf /etc/cni/net.d/{
  mkdir -p /etc/containerd/
  mv containerd-config.toml /etc/containerd/config.toml
  mv containerd.service /etc/systemd/system/
}{
  mv kubelet-config.yaml /var/lib/kubelet/
  mv kubelet.service /etc/systemd/system/
# Configure the Kubernetes Proxy
{
  mv kube-proxy-config.yaml /var/lib/kube-proxy/
  mv kube-proxy.service /etc/systemd/system/

2.6 Start the Worker Services

systemctl daemon-reloadsystemctl enable containerd kubelet kube-proxy
systemctl start containerd kubelet kube-proxy
systemctl status containerd kubelet kube-proxy

You may need to wait for a few seconds for the kubelet service to be fully operational.

Also check kernal logs for any errors regarding the file system:

2.7 Repeat for All Worker Nodes

Follow the same steps for both node-0 (EC2 A) and node-1(Raspberry Pi).

From the jumpbox, check if the worker nodes joined successfully:

ssh root@server \  "kubectl get nodes \
  --kubeconfig admin.kubeconfig"

You should see something like:

NAME     STATUS   ROLES    AGE    VERSIONnode-0   Ready    <none>   1m     v1.28.3
node-1   Ready    <none>   10s    v1.28.3

3. Configuring kubectl for Remote Access

We’ll set up a local kubeconfig for the user so you can run kubectl commands without specifying --kubeconfig each time.

You should be able to ping server.kubernetes.local based on the /etc/hosts DNS entry you configured previously:

curl -k --cacert ca.crt \  https://server.kubernetes.local:6443/version
{  "major": "1",  "gitVersion": "v1.32.0",
  "gitCommit": "70d3cc986aa8221cd1dfb1121852688902d3bf53",
  "gitTreeState": "clean",
  "buildDate": "2024-12-11T17:59:15Z",
  "goVersion": "go1.23.3",  "platform": "linux/arm64"

Generate a kubeconfig file suitable for authenticating as the admin user:

{  kubectl config set-cluster kubernetes-the-hard-way \
    --certificate-authority=ca.crt \
    --embed-certs=true \
    --server=https://server.kubernetes.local:6443
  kubectl config set-credentials admin \
    --client-certificate=admin.crt \
  kubectl config set-context kubernetes-the-hard-way \
    --cluster=kubernetes-the-hard-way \
  kubectl config use-context kubernetes-the-hard-way
}

Check the version of the remote Kubernetes cluster:

Client Version: v1.32.0Kustomize Version: v5.5.0

List the nodes in the remote Kubernetes cluster:

NAME     STATUS   ROLES    AGE     VERSIONnode-0   Ready    <none>   5m38s   v1.32.0
node-1   Ready    <none>   3m32s   v1.32.0

4. Provisioning Pod Network Routes

We’ll allow traffic from WireGuard (wg0) to the pod network (cni0) on each worker node. This ensures that pods on node-0 and node-1 can communicate back to the jumpbox or other hosts.

From your jumpbox machine, SSH into node-0:

iptables -A FORWARD -i wg0 -o cni0 -s 10.0.0.0/16 -d 10.244.1.0/24 -j ACCEPT
iptables -A FORWARD -i wg0 -o cni0 -s 10.0.0.0/16 -d 10.244.2.0/24 -j ACCEPT
These commands modify the firewall settings to allow traffic from the WireGuard interface wg0 to the container network interface cni0. This ensures that network packets can pass between the WireGuard network 10.0.0.0/16 and the Kubernetes pod networks on each node - 10.244.1.0/24 or 10.244.2.0/24.

We’ll confirm our cluster is functioning by creating a secret, deploying an Nginx app, and verifying logs and network connectivity.

From the jumpbox container, ensure the server container has bsdmainutils:

ssh root@server 'apt update && apt install -y bsdmainutils'
kubectl create secret generic kubernetes-the-hard-way \ --from-literal="mykey=mydata"

Print a hexdump of the kubernetes-the-hard-way secret stored in etcd:

ssh root@server \'etcdctl get /registry/secrets/default/kubernetes-the-hard-way | hexdump -C'
00000000  2f 72 65 67 69 73 74 72  79 2f 73 65 63 72 65 74  |/registry/secret|00000010  73 2f 64 65 66 61 75 6c  74 2f 6b 75 62 65 72 6e  |s/default/kubern|

Create an nginx deployment:

kubectl create deployment nginx \ --image=nginx:latest

List the pods created by the nginx deployment:

kubectl get pods -l app=nginx
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGEnginx-56fcf95486-c8dnx 1/1 Running 0 8s

Retrieve the full name of the nginx pod:

POD_NAME=$(kubectl get pods -l app=nginx \ -o jsonpath="{.items[0].metadata.name}")

Forward port 8080 on your local machine to port 80 of the nginx pod:

kubectl port-forward $POD_NAME 8080:80
Forwarding from 127.0.0.1:8080 -> 80Forwarding from [::1]:8080 -> 80

In a new terminal make an HTTP request using the forwarding address:

curl --head http://127.0.0.1:8080

You should see a response like this:

HTTP/1.1 200 OKServer: nginx/1.25.3
Date: Sun, 29 Oct 2023 01:44:32 GMT
Content-Type: text/htmlLast-Modified: Tue, 24 Oct 2023 13:46:47 GMTETag: "6537cac7-267"

Print the nginx pod logs:

...127.0.0.1 - - [01/Nov/2023:06:10:17 +0000] "HEAD / HTTP/1.1" 200 0 "-" "curl/7.88.1" "-"
kubectl exec -ti $POD_NAME -- nginx -v
nginx version: nginx/1.25.3

Expose the nginx deployment using a NodePort service:

kubectl create service nodeport nginx --tcp=80:80 --node-port=31703

Retrieve the NodePort assigned to the nginx service:

NODE_PORT=$(kubectl get svc nginx \ --output=jsonpath='{range .spec.ports[0]}{.nodePort}')

Make an HTTP request using the IP address and the nginx NodePort:

curl -I http://node-0:${NODE_PORT}
HTTP/1.1 200 OKServer: nginx/1.25.3
Date: Sun, 29 Oct 2023 05:11:15 GMT
Content-Type: text/htmlLast-Modified: Tue, 24 Oct 2023 13:46:47 GMTETag: "6537cac7-267"

If you’re done experimenting, remove containers and resources to avoid extra costs or clutter.

6.1 Stop & Remove Docker Containers

sudo docker stop jumpbox serversudo docker remove jumpbox server
sudo docker stop node-0sudo docker remove node-0
sudo docker stop node-1sudo docker remove node-1

Remove the /mnt directory from the node hosts:

6.3 Delete the EC2 Instances (Optional)

If you have creates the EC2 A and EC2 B just for the purposes of this guide, terminate them using the AWS Console.

Congratulations! You’ve completed your hybrid Kubernetes cluster across AWS EC2 and Raspberry Pi, with:

  • connectivity via WireGuard
  • and kubeconfigs for each cluster component
  • at rest in etcd
  • Control plane and worker nodes deployed manually
  • using an nginx smoke test

This concludes our step-by-step journey through “Kubernetes the Hard Way” with Docker containers, Raspberry Pi, and AWS EC2. We hope this deep dive has:

  1. Expanded your understanding of , networking, and security.
  2. Demonstrated how to etcd, the control plane, and worker nodes manually.
  3. Shown practical ways to and your cluster.

Feel free to explore additional features, add-ons, or further enhancements. Happy Kubernetes-ing!

items.title: Docker Interview Questions and Answers
items.author: Harikrishnan M
items.content:
Docker is an essential tool in modern DevOps and cloud computing. Below is a comprehensive list of important Docker interview questions and answers, ranging from basic to advanced, covering concepts, commands, and best practices.

Docker is a platform that enables developers to build, package, and deploy applications in containers. It provides lightweight, portable, and consistent environments across different computing environments.

2. What are the advantages of using Docker?

  • Portability: Runs consistently across different environments.
  • Efficiency: Uses fewer system resources than virtual machines.
  • Scalability: Easy to scale up or down based on demand.
  • Faster deployment: Speeds up CI/CD pipelines.

3. What is a Docker Container?

A Docker container is an isolated, lightweight, and portable runtime environment that includes everything needed to run an application (code, runtime, libraries, dependencies, and configurations).

4. What is a Docker Image?

A Docker image is a read-only template that contains the application and its dependencies. Containers are created from images.

# Pull an image from Docker Hubdocker pull nginx
# Run a container from the imagedocker run -d --name my_nginx -p 80:80 nginx

5. What is the difference between Docker Image and Container?

  • A is a blueprint used to create containers.
  • A is a running instance of an image.

Docker Hub is a cloud-based registry for sharing and distributing Docker images.

A Dockerfile is a script containing a set of instructions to automate the creation of a Docker image.

FROM ubuntu:latestRUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y nginx
COPY index.html /var/www/html/
CMD ["nginx", "-g", "daemon off;"]

8. Explain the lifecycle of a Docker container.

The lifecycle consists of:

  1.  — Build an image using docker build.
  2.  — Create and start a container using docker run.
  3.  — Temporarily stop and resume a running container.
  4.  — Gracefully stop a container using docker stop.
  5.  — Forcefully terminate a container using docker kill.
  6.  — Delete a container using docker rm.

9. What is the difference between CMD and ENTRYPOINT in a Dockerfile?

  • sets the default command but can be overridden at runtime.
  • is the main executable and is not easily overridden.
CMD ["echo", "Hello World"]ENTRYPOINT ["ping"]

10. How do you list running Docker containers?

docker ps  # Shows active containersdocker ps -a  # Shows all containers

11. How do you stop and remove a running container?

# Stop a containerdocker stop my_container
# Remove a containerdocker rm my_container

12. What is a Docker Volume?

A Docker Volume is a mechanism for persisting data between container restarts.

# Create and use a volumedocker volume create my_volume
docker run -d -v my_volume:/data nginx

13. What is the difference between a Volume and a Bind Mount?

  • is managed by Docker and stored in /var/lib/docker/volumes/.
  • maps a specific host directory to a container.

14. What is Docker Compose?

Docker Compose is a tool to define and run multi-container applications.

version: '3.8'services:    image: nginx      - "80:80"    image: mysql      MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD: root

15. What is the difference between Docker Swarm and Kubernetes?

  • : Native clustering and orchestration for Docker.
  • : More powerful, widely adopted container orchestration platform.

16. What is a Multi-Stage Build in Docker?

A multi-stage build reduces image size by separating the build process into different stages.

FROM golang:1.19 AS builderWORKDIR /appRUN go build -o myapp
FROM alpine:latestWORKDIR /root/
COPY --from=builder /app/myapp .
CMD ["./myapp"]

17. How do you scan Docker images for vulnerabilities?

You can use docker scan or tools like Trivy.

18. How do you restart a container automatically?

docker run -d --restart always nginx

19. How do you optimize Docker images?

  • Use lightweight base images (e.g., alpine).
  • Remove unnecessary dependencies.
  • Reduce the number of image layers.

20. How do you monitor Docker containers?

Use built-in commands and monitoring tools.

docker stats  # Real-time resource usagedocker logs my_container  # View logs

Mastering these Docker concepts and commands will help you excel in your DevOps interviews. Keep practicing with real-world scenarios and hands-on projects. Happy learning!

items.title: AWS MSK from Scratch: A Beginner’s Guide with Real-Time usecase
items.author: Harikrishnan M
items.content:
AWS Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka (MSK) is a fully managed service that makes it easy to build and run applications using Apache Kafka. Kafka is a popular open-source event streaming platform used for real-time data processing. AWS MSK removes the complexity of setting up and managing Kafka clusters, providing a scalable and reliable streaming solution.

In this guide, we’ll walk through AWS MSK from scratch, setting up a real-time streaming pipeline with a practical example. This tutorial is beginner-friendly and designed for those new to Kafka and AWS MSK.

Step 1: Understanding Kafka and AWS MSK

Apache Kafka is a distributed event streaming platform used for:

  • between applications
  • Real-time data processing
  • Event-driven architecture
  • and monitoring
  • (No manual cluster management)
  • (Handles large volumes of data seamlessly)
  • (Integration with AWS IAM and encryption features)
  • (Built-in Amazon CloudWatch integration)
  • (Automated backups and high availability)

Step 2: Setting Up AWS MSK Cluster

  • IAM permissions to create MSK clusters, EC2 instances, and security groups
  • Basic knowledge of AWS services like VPC, EC2, and IAM

2.1 Create a VPC and Subnets

AWS MSK requires a VPC with private subnets.

2.2 Create an AWS MSK Cluster

  1. Choose for full control
  • my-msk-cluster
  • kafka.t3.small (for testing)
  • 3 (minimum recommended for high availability)
  • Select the created VPC
  • Attach the previously created security group
  1. Click and wait for provisioning (~30 min)

2.3 Get the MSK Bootstrap Servers

Once the cluster is active:

  1. Go to AWS Console > MSK > Clusters
  2. Select the cluster and copy the  URL

Step 3: Setting Up Kafka Producer & Consumer

To test our MSK cluster, we will create a (sends messages) and a (receives messages).

3.1 Launch an EC2 Instance

  1. Launch a new EC2 instance with:
  • Allow SSH and Kafka ports (9092)
  • Create or use an existing key pair
  1. Connect to the instance via SSH:
ssh -i my-key.pem ec2-user@your-ec2-ip

3.2 Install Kafka CLI Tools

Run the following commands in the EC2 terminal:

sudo yum update -ywget https://archive.apache.org/dist/kafka/3.0.0/kafka_2.13-3.0.0.tgz
tar -xvzf kafka_2.13-3.0.0.tgz
cd kafka_2.13-3.0.0

3.3 Configure Kafka Connection to AWS MSK

Edit the config/client.properties file to include:

bootstrap.servers=<your-bootstrap-servers>security.protocol=SSL
ssl.truststore.location=/etc/pki/ca-trust/extracted/java/cacerts
ssl.truststore.password=changeit
bin/kafka-topics.sh --create --topic my-first-topic --bootstrap-server <your-bootstrap-servers> --partitions 1 --replication-factor 1 --command-config config/client.properties

Check if the topic is created:

bin/kafka-topics.sh --list --bootstrap-server <your-bootstrap-servers> --command-config config/client.properties

3.5 Start a Kafka Producer

bin/kafka-console-producer.sh --topic my-first-topic --bootstrap-server <your-bootstrap-servers> --producer.config config/client.properties

Type a message and press to send it.

3.6 Start a Kafka Consumer

Open another SSH session and run:

bin/kafka-console-consumer.sh --topic my-first-topic --bootstrap-server <your-bootstrap-servers> --from-beginning --consumer.config config/client.properties

You should see the messages sent by the producer!

Step 4: Integrating AWS Lambda with AWS MSK

AWS Lambda can process Kafka messages in real-time.

4.1 Create an IAM Role for Lambda

  1. Go to AWS Console > IAM > Roles
  2. Create a new role with AWSLambdaMSKExecutionRole permissions

4.2 Create a Lambda Function

  1. Go to AWS Console > Lambda > Create Function
  2. Select
  • KafkaMessageProcessor
  • Use the created IAM Role
  1. Add the following Python code:
import jsondef lambda_handler(event, context):
    for record in event['records']:
        for message in event['records'][record]:
            print("Message received:", message['value'])
    return {        'body': json.dumps('Processing complete')

4.3 Add MSK as an Event Source

  1. Go to
  2. Select and enter the topic details

AWS MSK simplifies Kafka cluster management and allows seamless event-driven processing with AWS Lambda. In this guide, we:

  • Set up an AWS MSK cluster
  • Created a Kafka producer and consumer
  • Configured security for Kafka clients
  • Integrated AWS Lambda with MSK

This setup is useful for real-time data pipelines, log processing, and event-driven applications. Start exploring AWS MSK today!

  • Experiment with different Kafka topics and partitions
  • Explore AWS Glue or Kinesis for data transformations
  • Implement security best practices with IAM and encryption

items.title: AWS Patch Manager
items.author: Harikrishnan M
items.content:
In the AWS DevOps landscape, maintaining the security and compliance of cloud-based environments is crucial. AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager is a fully managed service that automates the process of patching operating systems and applications for Amazon EC2 and on-premises instances. This blog will guide you through the entire process, from understanding the fundamentals to implementing patching in a real-world scenario.

What is AWS Patch Manager?

AWS Patch Manager is a feature of AWS Systems Manager (SSM) that helps automate the patching of Windows and Linux instances with security and software updates. It allows you to:

  • Define patch baselines to specify which patches should be applied.
  • Schedule patching using Maintenance Windows.
  • Use compliance reports to track patch status.
  • Automate patching workflows with AWS Lambda and CloudWatch Events.

Before we start configuring Patch Manager, ensure you have:

  1. with appropriate permissions.
  2. (Windows/Linux) with Systems Manager Agent (SSM Agent) installed.
  3. installed and configured.
  4. with necessary permissions to allow AWS Systems Manager to manage instances.
  5. defined to logically group instances for targeted patching

Step 1: Attach IAM Role to EC2 Instances

To allow Patch Manager to interact with your EC2 instances, you need to assign the appropriate IAM role.

Creating an IAM Role for SSM

  1. Navigate to → → .
  2. Choose and select .
  3. Attach the AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore policy.
  4. Name the role (e.g., SSMManagedEC2) and create it.
  5. Attach this role to your EC2 instances.

Step 2: Enable AWS Systems Manager

  1. Go to AWS Systems Manager Console
  2. Navigate to and verify that your instances appear in the list.
  3. If instances are not listed, ensure the SSM Agent is installed and running:
  • sudo amazon-linux-extras enable ssm-agent
  • sudo yum install -y amazon-ssm-agent
  • sudo systemctl start amazon-ssm-agent
  • sudo systemctl enable amazon-ssm-agent
  1. For Windows instances, install the SSM Agent manually or via PowerShell.

Step 3: Define a Patch Baseline

Patch baselines define which patches are approved for installation on your instances.

  1. Navigate to → .
  2. Click .
  3. Provide a name (e.g., Linux-Security-Patches).
  4. Select the (Amazon Linux, Ubuntu, Windows, etc.).
  5. Under , choose
  • Auto approve critical and security updates.
  • Specify the compliance level.
  1. Select an approval delay if needed.

Step 4: Register Patch Groups

Patch Groups help apply patches selectively to specific instances.

  1. Assign a tag to EC2 instances:
  • aws ec2 create-tags --resources i-xxxxxxxxxx --tags Key=PatchGroup,Value=WebServers
  1. In Patch Manager, link the to the tag (WebServers).
  2. Under , select and add the group name.

Step 5: Create a Maintenance Window

To schedule patching, configure a .

  1. Navigate to → .
  2. Click Create maintenance window.
  3. Provide a name (e.g., WebServersPatchWindow).
  4. Define the schedule (e.g., cron(0 3 ? * SUN *) for every Sunday at 3 AM).
  5. Select Allow unassociated targets and create the window.

Step 6: Register Targets to Maintenance Window

  1. Select the and choose .
  2. Define targets using the Patch Group tag (WebServers).

Step 7: Configure Patch Installation Task

  1. Select the and choose .
  2. Choose and select AWS-RunPatchBaseline.
  3. Set the as instances tagged with PatchGroup=WebServers.
  • Reboot if needed

Step 8: Manually Trigger Patch Installation (Optional)

If you want to manually install patches on demand, use the following command:

aws ssm send-command --document-name "AWS-RunPatchBaseline" --targets "Key=tag:PatchGroup,Values=WebServers" --parameters "Operation=Install" --region <your-region>

Step 9: Monitoring Patch Compliance

AWS provides compliance reports to track patch status.

  • Go to → → .
  • Run the following command to check compliance:
  • aws ssm list-compliance-summaries
  • Configure AWS CloudWatch logs to capture patching events.

Step 10: Automate Patch Reports using AWS Lambda

You can create an AWS Lambda function to send periodic patch compliance reports to an email or Slack channel.

  1. Create a new function and select or .
  2. Use to fetch compliance reports.
  3. Send data to Amazon SNS for notifications.

AWS Patch Manager simplifies and automates the patching of EC2 instances, ensuring security and compliance with minimal manual intervention. By following these steps, you can efficiently manage patching across your AWS infrastructure.

Start automating your patch management today and keep your infrastructure secure!

AWS Patch Manager was originally published in DevOps.dev on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

items.title: 9 Essential Automation Scripts Every DevOps Engineer Should Know
items.author: Harikrishnan M
items.content:
9 Essential Automation Scripts Every DevOps Engineers Should Know

Monitoring and logging are essential for system reliability. Whether you’re managing cloud infrastructure, microservices, or CI/CD pipelines, automation scripts help prevent downtime and operational failures. In this blog, I’ll share 9 essential automation scripts that every DevOps engineer should know. These scripts will help you monitor system performance, detect anomalies, and ensure smooth application functioning.

This guide is useful for both beginners and experienced DevOps professionals. Let’s explore these scripts, their implementation, and real-world scenarios where they provide value.

1. Disk Space Monitoring Script

Running out of disk space can lead to application crashes and failed deployments.

A Bash script checks disk usage and sends an alert if it exceeds a threshold.

#!/bin/bashTHRESHOLD=85
df -h | awk '{if($5+0 > '$THRESHOLD') print $0}'

A logging service storing large volumes of data experiences slow performance due to insufficient disk space. This script provides early warnings so that additional storage can be provisioned before reaching critical limits.

2. CPU Usage Monitoring Script

High CPU usage can indicate an unoptimized application or excessive resource consumption.

A Python script monitors CPU usage and sends alerts when it crosses a defined threshold.

import psutilif psutil.cpu_percent(interval=1) > 85:
    print("High CPU Usage Alert!")

A machine learning inference server experiences high CPU usage due to multiple simultaneous requests. This script helps identify resource spikes, allowing auto-scaling strategies to be implemented.

3. Log Rotation Automation Script

Large logs can consume disk space and make debugging difficult.

A Bash script compresses and archives old logs, keeping only recent ones.

#!/bin/bashLOG_DIR="/var/log/app/"
tar -czf $LOG_DIR/archive_$(date +%F).tar.gz $LOG_DIR/*.log
rm $LOG_DIR/*.log

A video processing application generates massive logs. This script ensures that logs are archived periodically, preventing performance degradation due to excessive file accumulation.

4. Service Uptime Monitoring Script

Immediate detection of service downtime minimizes disruption.

A Python script pings a service endpoint and alerts if it’s unreachable.

import requeststry:
    response = requests.get("http://api.your-service.com")
    if response.status_code != 200:
        print("Service Down Alert!")
except Exception:
    print("Service Unreachable!")

A real-time stock trading platform depends on API services. This script alerts engineers if an API is unresponsive, allowing them to take immediate action.

5. Memory Usage Monitoring Script

Low memory availability can cause system crashes.

A Bash script checks memory usage and alerts if it falls below a threshold.

#!/bin/bashTHRESHOLD=200 # in MB
AVAILABLE=$(free -m | awk '/Mem/ {print $7}')
if [ $AVAILABLE -lt $THRESHOLD ]; thenfi

A containerized AI inference engine occasionally runs out of memory due to unpredictable request patterns. This script provides early warnings to allocate additional resources or restart services before failures occur.

6. Docker Container Health Check Script

Monitoring container health ensures the stability of microservices.

A Bash script checks the health status of running Docker containers.

#!/bin/bashfor container in $(docker ps --format "{{.Names}}"); do
    STATUS=$(docker inspect --format '{{.State.Health.Status}}' $container)
    echo "Container: $container - Status: $STATUS"

An e-commerce backend runs multiple microservices in Docker. This script quickly identifies failing containers, allowing prompt intervention.

7. Kubernetes Pod Monitoring Script

Pods are the smallest deployable units in Kubernetes, and their health determines application stability.

This script checks for non-running pods.

#!/bin/bashkubectl get pods --all-namespaces | grep -v 'Running'

A ride-sharing app experiences pod failures due to node resource exhaustion. This script identifies the failing pods, enabling immediate troubleshooting.

8. Website Availability Check Script

Websites going offline can lead to business loss.

A Python script checks if a website is up and alerts if it’s down.

import requeststry:
    response = requests.get("http://yourwebsite.com")
    print("Website is up!") if response.status_code == 200 else print("Website is down!")    print("Website Unreachable!")

A news platform ensures its main website remains accessible during peak hours using this script.

9. Automated Backup Verification Script

Backups must be verified to ensure data safety.

A Bash script verifies backup integrity.

#!/bin/bashBACKUP_DIR="/backups"
for file in $BACKUP_DIR/*.tar.gz; do
    if ! tar -tzf $file > /dev/null; then
        echo "Corrupted backup file: $file"
    fi

A financial firm automates backup verification to prevent data loss in case of system failures.

These scripts automate essential DevOps tasks, improving system reliability and reducing manual efforts. By integrating them into your infrastructure, you can proactively monitor and manage your cloud and on-premise environments.

Have you implemented automation scripts in your workflow? Share your experiences and insights in the comments below!

items.title: DevOps & AWS Automation with Go: Simplifying Metrics & Daily Operations
items.author: Harikrishnan M
items.content:
In the fast-paced world of DevOps, automation is key to efficiency and scalability. While scripting with Bash and Python is common, has emerged as a powerful alternative due to its speed, concurrency, and simplicity. When working with AWS, Go enables engineers to automate repetitive tasks, monitor system metrics, and optimize cloud resources effectively.

This blog explores how Go can streamline AWS automation, offering practical examples of gathering metrics, managing resources, and handling day-to-day operations.

Why Use Go for DevOps & AWS Automation?

  1. Go is compiled, making it faster than interpreted languages like Python.
  2. Goroutines allow efficient multi-threaded execution, crucial for handling multiple AWS resources simultaneously.
  3. Statically typed and designed with a focus on simplicity and maintainability.
  4. A well-documented SDK enables seamless interaction with AWS services.
  5. Go binaries run on Linux, macOS, and Windows without dependencies.

Use Cases of AWS Automation with Go

1. Automating CloudWatch Metrics Collection

Monitoring AWS services is a critical part of DevOps operations. Instead of manually checking metrics, we can automate this using Go and AWS SDK.

Example: Fetching EC2 CPU Utilization from CloudWatch

import (	"context"	"time"
	"github.com/aws/aws-sdk-go-v2/config"	"github.com/aws/aws-sdk-go-v2/service/cloudwatch"
	"github.com/aws/aws-sdk-go-v2/service/cloudwatch/types"
)
func main() {	cfg, err := config.LoadDefaultConfig(context.TODO())		panic("Failed to load AWS configuration")
	cwClient := cloudwatch.NewFromConfig(cfg)	metricData, err := cwClient.GetMetricStatistics(context.TODO(), &cloudwatch.GetMetricStatisticsInput{
		Namespace:  aws.String("AWS/EC2"),
		MetricName: aws.String("CPUUtilization"),
		Dimensions: []types.Dimension{
			{
				Name:  aws.String("InstanceId"),
				Value: aws.String("i-0dcf45d30916cb201"),		},
		Period:     aws.Int32(300),
		StartTime:  aws.Time(time.Now().Add(-1 * time.Hour)),
		EndTime:    aws.Time(time.Now()),
		Statistics: []types.Statistic{types.StatisticAverage},
	if err != nil {		panic(err)
	for _, datapoint := range metricData.Datapoints {		fmt.Printf("CPU Utilization: %.2f%% at %v\n", *datapoint.Average, datapoint.Timestamp)}

2. Automating AWS EC2 Instance Management

Starting and stopping AWS EC2 instances manually is time-consuming. Automate it with Go!

Example: Start/Stop an EC2 Instance

	"github.com/aws/aws-sdk-go-v2/aws"	"github.com/aws/aws-sdk-go-v2/config"
	"github.com/aws/aws-sdk-go-v2/service/ec2"
)
func main() {	cfg, err := config.LoadDefaultConfig(context.TODO())		panic("Failed to load AWS configuration")	ec2Client := ec2.NewFromConfig(cfg)
	instanceID := "i-0dcf45d30916cb201"
	_, err = ec2Client.StartInstances(context.TODO(), &ec2.StartInstancesInput{		InstanceIds: []string{instanceID},
	if err != nil {		panic(err)
	fmt.Println("Instance started successfully!")}

Similarly, modify StartInstances to StopInstances to stop the instance.

3. Automating AWS S3 Bucket Operations

AWS S3 is commonly used for storage. Here’s how you can automate listing objects in a bucket.

	"github.com/aws/aws-sdk-go-v2/config"	"github.com/aws/aws-sdk-go-v2/service/s3"
func main() {	cfg, err := config.LoadDefaultConfig(context.TODO())		panic("Failed to load AWS configuration")
	s3Client := s3.NewFromConfig(cfg)	bucketName := "my-automation-bucket"
	output, err := s3Client.ListObjectsV2(context.TODO(), &s3.ListObjectsV2Input{
		Bucket: &bucketName,
	if err != nil {		panic(err)
	for _, item := range output.Contents {		fmt.Println("Found object: ", *item.Key)}

Best Practices for Go-based AWS Automation

  • for authentication instead of storing AWS credentials in your code.
  • in Go to perform multiple AWS tasks simultaneously.
  • Monitor failures and retries using proper error handling and logging.
  • for event-driven automation.
  • to avoid unnecessary API costs.

Using Golang for AWS automation allows DevOps engineers to optimize their workflows, improve efficiency, and reduce operational overhead. Whether it’s monitoring metrics, managing EC2 instances, or automating S3 operations, Go provides a robust and scalable solution for cloud automation.

If you’re still scripting daily tasks manually, it’s time to explore Go-powered AWS automation and make DevOps more efficient!

items.title: Redis Unlocked: A Deep Dive into Persistence, Clustering, and Distributed Patterns
items.author: Amber Liu
items.content:
Redis is much more than a simple, fast key–value store. You probably know it for its in-memory design and single-threaded event loop, which make it incredibly quick for many use cases. But what about the details? How does it manage persistence, scale under heavy load, or handle distributed system challenges like caching, rate limiting, and leader election? And what pitfalls — like hot keys, replication lag, and memory management issues — should you be aware of?

In this article, we’ll take a deeper look at these questions:

  • Q1: What are the differences between Redis Standalone and Redis Cluster in terms of working mechanism, structure, communication protocol, and failover?
  • Q2: How does Redis persist data, and what are the options for backup and restore?
  • Q3: What are some known issues with Redis and how can they be addressed (with trade-offs)?
  • Q4: What are Redis’s capabilities for distributed system patterns?
  • Q5: How does Redis handle memory management and access control?

Q1: What are the differences between Redis Standalone and Redis Cluster in terms of working mechanism, structure, communication protocol, and failover?

In a standalone setup, you have a single primary Redis instance that holds all your data. You can also have replicas (or slaves) that copy everything from the primary. Think of it as one main library (the master) with several branch libraries (the replicas) that hold identical collections. This model primarily scales vertically — by beefing up the primary — or by adding replicas to handle more read requests.

Diagram: Standalone Setup

          +-------------------+          |   Redis Master    |          +---------+---------+       +------------+------------+
       |                         |
  +----v----+               +----v----+
  | Replica |               | Replica |
  | (Slave) |               | (Slave) |
  +---------+               +---------+

A Redis Cluster is designed for larger-scale systems. Instead of one node holding all the data, the dataset is split into 16,384 hash slots. Each master node in the cluster is responsible for a portion of these slots, meaning no single master has the whole picture. Every master can also have one or more replicas for redundancy. This lets you scale horizontally by adding more masters (with their replicas) to distribute both read and write loads.

                        +---------------------------+                        |       Redis Cluster       |
                        +---------------------------+
                                   /   \                   +--------------+       +--------------+
                   |                                 |
             +-----v-----+                     +-----v-----+
             | Master A  |                     | Master B  |
             | (Slots 0-8191)                  | (Slots 8192-16383)
             +-----+-----+                     +-----+-----+
                   |                                   |
          +--------+--------+                 +--------+--------+
          |                 |                 |                 |
      +---v---+         +---v---+         +---v---+         +---v---+
      | Slave A1|       | Slave A2|       | Slave B1|       | Slave B2|
      +---------+       +---------+       +---------+       +---------+

(2) Communication Between Nodes

The communication here is pretty straightforward. The master simply pushes updates directly to each of its replicas. There’s no extra protocol or dedicated channel because it’s just copying the complete dataset from one node to the others.

Redis Cluster is a bit more complex. Each node uses two TCP ports:

  • Client Port: Where clients connect and send commands.
  • Cluster Bus Port: Typically set as the client port plus 10,000 (e.g., if the client port is 7000, the cluster bus port is 17000). This port is reserved solely for inter-node communication.

Nodes exchange information using a gossip protocol. In simple terms, they regularly send updates about which nodes are online, what hash slots each master covers, and whether any node has failed. This continuous exchange keeps every node up to date on the cluster’s overall state.

Standalone (with Sentinel)

When you run a standalone Redis with replicas, you usually pair it with Redis Sentinel. Sentinel monitors the master and, if it goes down, automatically promotes one of the replicas to be the new master. This allows your system to recover without manual intervention.

In a cluster, failover is handled within the cluster itself. The nodes constantly exchange information via the cluster bus. If a master fails, its replicas hold an election to choose a new master. Once elected, the cluster updates its hash slot mapping so that the new master takes over the responsibilities of the old one — all without needing an external tool like Sentinel.

Q2: How Does Redis Persist Data, and What Are the Options for Backup and Restore?

Below is a diagram summarizing Redis’s persistence options, followed by detailed explanations of each method along with backup and restore commands.

(1)Diagram of Persistence Options

                   +-------------------------------------+                   |       Redis Persistence Options     |
                   | (Configured via redis.conf settings)|
                   +-----------------+-------------------+            +------------------------+------------------------+
            |                                                 |
      +-----v-----+                                      +----v-----+
      |   AOF     |                                      |   RDB    |
      | (appendonly.aof)                                 | (dump.rdb)|
      +-----------+                                      +----------+
            \                                                 /
             \                                               /
              \                                             /
                \             Hybrid Persistence          /
                 \         (Both AOF and RDB Enabled)      /
                  \                                         /
                   +---------------------------------------+

A. AOF (Append-Only File) Persistence

When you set appendonly yes, Redis logs every write operation to an AOF file. If Redis crashes, it can rebuild your dataset by replaying these logged commands. You can configure how frequently Redis flushes the AOF buffer:

  • appendfsync always: Flushes on every command (most durable, but slowest).
  • appendfsync everysec: Flushes every second (good balance).
  • appendfsync no: Relies on the OS, which can be faster but less predictable.

Enabling AOF means extra disk I/O, which can slow performance under heavy loads.

B. RDB (Redis Database) Snapshots

RDB snapshots create point-in-time dumps of your dataset. They’re triggered by save directives in your redis.conf (for example, save 900 1 means if at least 1 key changes in 900 seconds, a snapshot is taken). The resulting file (usually dump.rdb) is compact and fast to load, but you might lose recent data if a crash occurs between snapshots.

Many Redis setups enable both AOF and RDB. This means:

  • Every write is logged in the AOF for detailed recovery.
  • Periodic RDB snapshots provide a full backup. On restart, Redis typically loads data from the AOF (as it’s more up-to-date) but can fall back on the RDB snapshot if needed.
  • AOF Only:Disable RDB by commenting out the save directives if you want maximum durability.
  • RDB Only:Set appendonly no if you prefer performance and can tolerate some data loss between snapshots.

(2) Backup and Restore Commands

  1. Trigger a snapshot with:redis-cli BGSAVE
  2. Once completed, copy the RDB file:cp /var/lib/redis/dump.rdb /backup/dump.rdb
  • Simply copy the AOF file:cp /var/lib/redis/appendonly.aof /backup/appendonly.aof
  1. Stop Redis:sudo systemctl stop redis
  2. Replace the current RDB file with your backup:cp /backup/dump.rdb /var/lib/redis/dump.rdb
  3. Restart Redis:sudo systemctl start redis
  • Follow similar steps as above. If the AOF is corrupted, use redis-check-aof to repair it before restarting.

Q3: What Are Some Known Issues with Redis and How Can They Be Addressed (with Trade-offs)?

Redis is fast and simple, but it has a few quirks. Here are some common issues and how you might tackle them.

The Problem:Sometimes, one key becomes extremely popular — a counter or frequently accessed cache entry — and overloads the node handling it. In a cluster, if that key lands on a single shard, it creates a bottleneck.

  • Key Sharding:Divide a hot key into several smaller keys (like counter:1, counter:2, etc.) and aggregate the results. More complex application logic.
  • Client-Side Caching/Rate Limiting:Cache values on the client side or limit the request rate for that key. Requires careful management of cache invalidation and throttling.

The Problem:In master–slave setups, heavy write loads can cause replicas to lag behind the master, meaning reads from replicas might be slightly stale.

  • Tuning Replication Settings:Adjust buffer sizes and timeouts to reduce lag. May increase resource usage.
  • Synchronous Writes/Direct Master Reads:Use commands like WAIT or direct critical reads to the master. Increases latency and load on the master.

(3) Single-Threaded Blocking Operations

The Problem:Redis processes commands on a single thread. Heavy operations (like long Lua scripts or large KEYS scans) can block other operations.

  • Optimize or Avoid Heavy Operations:Refactor or split up intensive tasks. Some heavy operations might be unavoidable.
  • Pipelining or Offloading Work:Use pipelining to send multiple commands at once, or offload heavy tasks to another system. Adds complexity to your application design.

(4) Memory Management and Fragmentation

The Problem:Since Redis is in-memory, heavy writes and deletions can lead to memory fragmentation, reducing efficiency.

  • Eviction Policies:Use policies like LRU, LFU, or TTL-based eviction to manage memory. Some keys might be evicted unintentionally.
  • Regular Maintenance:Periodically rewrite the AOF file or defragment memory. These tasks consume resources and must be scheduled carefully.

(5) Cluster Management and Scaling

The Problem:Running Redis in cluster mode introduces additional complexity — managing hash slots, node rebalancing, and failover can be challenging.

  • Use Management Tools and Monitoring:Employ tools or operators to automate slot rebalancing and node failure handling. These tools add extra layers to your infrastructure and can be potential points of failure.

Q4: What Are Redis’s Capabilities for Distributed System Patterns?

Redis is much more than a simple key–value store. Its features support various distributed system patterns that are essential in modern applications.

Overview:Caching stores frequently accessed data in memory for quick retrieval. Redis shines at this, with several common approaches:

  • Cache-Aside (Lazy Loading):The application checks Redis first; on a miss, it fetches data from the primary datastore, caches it, and returns it. A website that stores user profiles in Redis, loading from a SQL database only if the data isn’t in cache.
  • Read-Through and Write-Through:The cache automatically fetches data from the database (read-through), or writes updates to both the cache and database simultaneously (write-through). An e-commerce platform that keeps product inventory updated in both systems.
  • Write-Behind (Write-Back):Updates go to the cache and are flushed to the database asynchronously. A logging system that needs to quickly record events before persisting them.

Challenges:Cache invalidation — making sure your cache stays in sync with your primary datastore — is a common challenge that may be handled via TTLs, pub/sub mechanisms, or manual triggers.

Overview:Distributed locks prevent multiple nodes from modifying the same resource simultaneously.

How It Works:Using commands like SET with NX (set if not exists) and an expiry, Redis can manage locks. The RedLock algorithm, for instance, extends this concept across multiple Redis instances for better fault tolerance.

Example:Microservices processing orders can use a distributed lock to ensure that each order is handled by only one service at a time.

Trade-offs:Locks introduce overhead and potential delays. They must be carefully managed to handle issues like network partitions or clock drift.

(3) Rate Limiting & Concurrency Control

Overview:Rate limiting prevents any single user or process from overloading your system. Concurrency control helps manage how many tasks run simultaneously.

Implementation:Using sorted sets (to record timestamps) and Lua scripts (for atomic operations) in Redis, you can enforce limits such as “100 requests per minute per user.”

Example:An API gateway might use Redis to block requests when a user exceeds the allowed rate.

Trade-offs:These solutions add complexity and require fine-tuning to strike the right balance between user experience and system protection.

(4) Leader Election and Coordination

Overview:Leader election is critical when one node needs to coordinate tasks among many.

How It Works:Simple techniques — like setting a key with NX and an expiry — can elect a leader. Once a node secures the “leader lock,” it takes charge of tasks like scheduling or coordinating state changes.

Example:In a distributed cron job system, only one instance should trigger a job at any given time. Redis can help determine which instance becomes the leader.

Considerations:While simple and effective, these methods might not suit every high-stakes coordination scenario without additional safeguards.

Overview:Redis has built-in geospatial commands for proximity searches, making it ideal for location-based services.

How It Works:Commands such as GEOADD, GEORADIUS, and GEODIST let you store and query location data efficiently.

Example:A mobile app can quickly find nearby restaurants by storing each restaurant’s location and then querying for all locations within a given radius of the user’s current position.

Limitations:Redis is great for basic proximity searches, but for complex geospatial queries (like polygon searches), you might need a dedicated search engine or the RediSearch module.

Q5: How Does Redis Handle Memory Management and Access Control?

Since Redis keeps everything in RAM, its speed is incredible — but you have to manage memory carefully.

  • Redis relies on efficient data structures, but as your data grows, monitoring memory usage becomes vital.
  • When memory runs low, Redis can automatically remove keys. Common policies include:
  • LRU (Least Recently Used): Removes keys that haven’t been accessed for a while.
  • LFU (Least Frequently Used): Removes keys that are accessed less often.
  • TTL-based Eviction: Expires keys based on a pre-set time-to-live.

Choosing the right policy depends on your use case — if you’re caching web pages, for instance, LRU might be the best choice.

  • You can adjust Redis configuration settings to optimize memory use. Monitoring tools like the INFO command or external systems help you watch for issues like memory fragmentation or inefficient usage.

(2) Access Control and Security

  • Authentication:Redis supports password-based authentication via the requirepass directive. This offers a basic level of security, but you usually need more in production.
  • Protected Mode:When enabled, Redis only allows connections from localhost by default.
  • Network Segmentation:It’s crucial to prevent Redis from being directly exposed to the internet. Use firewalls, VPNs, or other methods to restrict access.

items.title: Spring Boot and gRPC: A Faster Alternative to REST?
items.author: Swapnil Kalbande
items.content:
In distributed systems and microservices, RESTful APIs have been the dominant standard for communication. However, as applications scale and require lower latency, better performance, and efficient data exchange, gRPC (Google Remote Procedure Call) is emerging as a powerful alternative.

This blog explores why gRPC is gaining traction, its advantages over REST, and how to implement gRPC in a Spring Boot application.

Why is gRPC Gaining Popularity?

gRPC, built on HTTP/2, provides several advantages that make it appealing for modern applications:

  1. High Performance & Low Latency: Uses HTTP/2 multiplexing to send multiple requests over a single connection, reducing latency.
  2. : Uses Protocol Buffers (Protobuf) for compact and faster serialization compared to JSON.
  3. : Supports real-time communication with streaming capabilities.
  4. : Uses IDL (Interface Definition Language) to define APIs, reducing errors in communication.
  5. Automatic Code Generation: Generates client and server stubs, minimizing boilerplate code.

REST vs gRPC: A Comparative Analysis

When to Choose gRPC Over REST?

While REST is still widely used, gRPC is more beneficial in the following scenarios:

  • Microservices Communication: Efficient service-to-service communication with lower network overhead.
  • : Ideal for applications requiring bi-directional data flow, such as chat applications.
  • : Optimized for low-bandwidth environments due to compact Protobuf messages.
  • : Supports multiple languages, making it suitable for polyglot environments.

Implementing gRPC with Spring Boot

Add the following dependencies to your pom.xml for Spring Boot and gRPC support:

<dependencies>    <!-- gRPC dependencies -->        <groupId>io.grpc</groupId>
        <artifactId>grpc-spring-boot-starter</artifactId>
        <version>2.14.0.RELEASE</version>    <dependency>
        <groupId>com.google.protobuf</groupId>
        <artifactId>protobuf-java</artifactId>
        <version>3.21.12</version>
    </dependency>

Step 2: Define the gRPC Service

Create a .proto file to define the service contract:

syntax = "proto3";
option java_multiple_files = true;
option java_package = "com.example.grpc";
service GreetingService {
    rpc SayHello (HelloRequest) returns (HelloResponse);message HelloRequest {}    string message = 1;

Compile the .proto file using the Protobuf compiler to generate Java classes.

Step 3: Implement the gRPC Server in Spring Boot

Create a service implementation extending GreetingServiceGrpc.GreetingServiceImplBase:

import io.grpc.stub.StreamObserver;import net.devh.boot.grpc.server.service.GrpcService;public class GreetingServiceImpl extends GreetingServiceGrpc.GreetingServiceImplBase {    public void sayHello(HelloRequest request, StreamObserver<HelloResponse> responseObserver) {
        String message = "Hello, " + request.getName();
        HelloResponse response = HelloResponse.newBuilder().setMessage(message).build();
        responseObserver.onNext(response);
        responseObserver.onCompleted();}

Step 4: Implement the gRPC Client

Create a gRPC client to call the service:

import io.grpc.ManagedChannel;import io.grpc.ManagedChannelBuilder;
public class GrpcClient {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ManagedChannel channel = ManagedChannelBuilder.forAddress("localhost", 9090).usePlaintext().build();
        GreetingServiceGrpc.GreetingServiceBlockingStub stub = GreetingServiceGrpc.newBlockingStub(channel);
        HelloResponse response = stub.sayHello(HelloRequest.newBuilder().setName("John").build());
        System.out.println(response.getMessage());
        channel.shutdown();}

Step 5: Running the Application

  1. Start the Spring Boot application to run the gRPC server.
  2. Run the GrpcClient to call the service and receive a response.

gRPC provides a powerful and efficient alternative to REST, especially for microservices, streaming, and real-time applications. With Spring Boot’s gRPC support, integrating it into modern applications is seamless.

Should you use gRPC over REST? The answer depends on your use case. If performance, efficiency, and real-time communication are key concerns, gRPC is worth considering.

Do you use gRPC in your projects? Share your experience in the comments!

items.title: How to Install Grafana and Prometheus on Kubernetes Without Paying for a Load Balancer
items.author: Muhammad Abdullah
items.content:
When setting up monitoring and logging on Kubernetes, many guides assume you’ll use a cloud provider’s load balancer, which can be costly. However, if you already have a domain and want to deploy Grafana, Prometheus, and Loki without incurring additional expenses for a load balancer, this guide is for you.

  • A Kubernetes cluster (self-managed or cloud-based)
  • A domain name already set up with external access
  • kubectl and helm installed
  • An Ingress controller (like NGINX Ingress Controller) running in your cluster

Grafana is used for visualization and will be exposed via Ingress.

helm repo add grafana https://grafana.github.io/helm-chartshelm install grafana grafana/grafana -n monitoring \
  --set service.type=ClusterIP

After installation, go to the config map in the monitoring namespace and replace this data with your domain data.

data:  grafana.ini: |    check_for_updates = true    url = https://grafana.net    mode = console    data = /var/lib/grafana/    plugins = /var/lib/grafana/plugins
    provisioning = /etc/grafana/provisioning
    [server]
    domain = 'yourdomain.com/grafana'
    root_url = 'https://yourdomain.com/grafana'
    serve_from_sub_path = true

Create a ingress in monitoring namespace to acccess it

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1kind: Ingress  name: grafana-ingress  annotations:
    kubernetes.io/ingress.class: nginx
    cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer: letsencrypt-prod
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "false"
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/from-to-www-redirect: "true"  rules:    http:      - path: /grafana        backend:            name: grafana              number: 3000

Step 2: Install Prometheus

helm repo add prometheus-community https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-chartshelm install my-prometheus prometheus-community/prometheus -n monitoring

Add connection string in grafana

http://prometheus-server.monitoring.svc.cluster.local:80
